BuildyBrick has engaged four specialist subcontractors for utilities, structural works, interior fit-out, and underground parking excavation. The CEO of BuildyBrick has suggested that all four subcontractors should be given permanent seats on the project board as Senior Suppliers to ensure direct lines of communication. The Project Executive is concerned this will make the board unwieldy. Which approach BEST reflects PRINCE2 guidance for managing this commercial situation?

options.A: Accept the CEO of BuildyBrick's proposal, as PRINCE2 requires all suppliers engaged on a project to be represented on the project board to ensure complete integration of direction and decision-making across the supply chain.
options.B: The CEO of BuildyBrick, already acting as Senior Supplier, should represent all four subcontractors on the project board, as it is typically more effective for the contracts manager or prime contractor to sit on the board on behalf of multiple suppliers when there are more than three or four.
options.C: Appoint the four subcontractors as additional Senior Suppliers but allow them to attend only the portions of project board meetings relevant to their specific work packages, mirroring the approach used by BuildyBrick for design reviews.
options.D: Remove the CEO of BuildyBrick from the project board and replace them with a single representative jointly nominated by all four subcontractors, as the prime contractor role is no longer needed once subcontractors are directly engaged.

rationale: Option A is incorrect. PRINCE2 guidance explicitly states that if there are more than three or four suppliers, it is typically more effective for the contracts manager or prime contractor responsible for the performance of all supplier contracts to sit on the project board on their behalf, rather than all suppliers being represented. This avoids an unwieldy board. Option B is correct. PRINCE2 7th Edition Section 6.4.2 states: 'if there are more than three or four suppliers, then it will be typically more effective for the contracts manager responsible for the performance of all the supplier contracts to sit on the project board on their behalf. Or it may be appropriate to appoint a prime contractor.' BuildyBrick is the appointed main contractor and the CEO already holds the Senior Supplier role; they are best placed to represent the subcontractors, maintaining a manageable board size while preserving clear governance. Option C is incorrect. While the scenario booklet references a partial-attendance arrangement for BuildyBrick during the design stage, that was a temporary measure for a specific technical review purpose. PRINCE2 guidance does not recommend granting all subcontractors seats but allowing partial attendance as a standard solution to board size; the preferred approach where multiple suppliers exist is prime contractor representation. Option D is incorrect. Removing the CEO of BuildyBrick, who is the representative of the main contractor and holds contractual accountability, undermines the clear line of accountability from the project board to the supply chain. PRINCE2 requires the project board to maintain clear governance, and the prime contractor relationship is central to this in a multi-supplier commercial context.

topic: Plans - Stages, Planning Horizon & Stage Boundaries
question: The Project Manager for LouisShopping is preparing the Stage 3 plan (site preparation, utilities, construction). Stage 3 is expected to span over two financial years, making it the longest and most complex stage. The Portfolio Director has requested that the project align stage boundaries with the end of each financial year to allow portfolio-level budget accounting. The Project Manager is assessing how to respond to this request. Which course of action BEST aligns with PRINCE2 guidance?

options.A: Reject the Portfolio Director's request entirely, as PRINCE2 requires stage boundaries to be determined solely by the project board based on the level of risk and complexity of the project, not by external organizational financial cycles.
options.B: Split Stage 3 into sub-stages aligned with each financial year end, documenting this tailoring decision in the project initiation documentation, as PRINCE2 recognises that stage boundaries may need to align with external events such as budget cycles.
options.C: Retain Stage 3 as a single stage but produce a detailed two-year project plan to satisfy the Portfolio Director's reporting needs, as PRINCE2 mandates that the project plan must always be produced at the level of detail required by external stakeholders.
options.D: Request an exception plan to cover the entire two-year construction period, as the extended duration of Stage 3 represents a forecast breach of the stage's time tolerance that requires escalation to the project board.

rationale: Option A is incorrect. While the project board has primary authority over stage boundaries, PRINCE2 guidance in Section 7.2.3 explicitly acknowledges that stage boundaries may need to align with external events, stating: 'In organizations with an annual budget cycle, there may be a requirement to provide an accounting of what the project has delivered and also what is in progress as it stands at the end of the budget year prior to authorization of the next portion of the project budget.' Rejecting the request outright ignores this valid organisational context. Option B is correct. PRINCE2 Section 7.2.3.2 identifies 'Appropriate decision points' and 'Alignment with programme activities' as legitimate drivers of stage length and boundary placement. Aligning stage boundaries with financial year ends is a recognised and supported reason to structure stages, and the decision to do so should be documented as part of tailoring within the project initiation documentation. This also provides the project board with natural control points over a lengthy and complex stage, improving governance. Option C is incorrect. PRINCE2 does not mandate that the project plan must always be produced at the level of detail required by external stakeholders. The project plan is a high-level document covering the full lifecycle; detailed planning is reserved for stage plans within the planning horizon. Producing a fully detailed two-year plan would likely be inaccurate and is contrary to the planning horizon principle described in Section 7.2.1. Option D is incorrect. An exception plan is produced in response to a forecast or actual breach of an agreed tolerance for a current stage, not as a proactive planning tool for future long-duration stages. The scenario describes a planning decision, not a tolerance breach, so an exception plan is not the appropriate mechanism here.

topic: Organizing - Role Integration, Programme Context & Sustainability
question: The 'Louistown is Open for Business' (LOB) portfolio includes several projects running concurrently. The Portfolio Director is considering how to integrate the governance of LouisShopping with two other projects in the portfolio. They are also under pressure from the Mayor to formally embed Louistown's 'green town' sustainability obligations into project accountability. Which TWO actions are MOST consistent with PRINCE2 guidance on programme integration and sustainability governance? Select the answer that contains BOTH correct actions.

options.A: Action 1: Appoint the Portfolio Director as the Project Executive for LouisShopping, as PRINCE2 states the programme manager may serve as the project executive for one or more projects. Action 2: Document sustainability targets, including the 'green town' obligations, in the business case, product descriptions, and role descriptions to embed clear accountability across the project ecosystem.
options.B: Action 1: Establish a single project board to direct all LOB portfolio projects collectively, as PRINCE2 requires a single directing body when multiple projects share the same executive. Action 2: Assign sustainability accountability exclusively to the Sustainability Manager acting as Project Assurance, as this role is specifically responsible for environmental compliance assurance.
options.C: Action 1: Appoint the Portfolio Director as the Project Executive for LouisShopping, as PRINCE2 states the programme manager may serve as the project executive for one or more projects. Action 2: Assign sustainability accountability exclusively to the Sustainability Manager acting as Project Assurance, as this role is specifically responsible for environmental compliance assurance.
options.D: Action 1: Establish a single project board to direct all LOB portfolio projects collectively, as PRINCE2 requires a single directing body when multiple projects share the same executive. Action 2: Document sustainability targets, including the 'green town' obligations, in the business case, product descriptions, and role descriptions to embed clear accountability across the project ecosystem.

rationale: Option A is correct. Action 1 is directly supported by PRINCE2 Section 6.4.1, which states: 'The programme manager may be the project executive for one or more of the projects.' The Portfolio Director overseeing the LOB portfolio is fulfilling an equivalent programme management function, and assuming the Project Executive role for LouisShopping is an explicitly sanctioned integration mechanism. Action 2 is directly supported by Section 6.4.4, which states: 'there should be clear accountability for sustainability targets across the project ecosystem captured in the business case, the requirement setting, and people's role descriptions.' Embedding sustainability across multiple management products and role descriptions is the PRINCE2-recommended approach. Option B is incorrect. Action 1 is wrong because PRINCE2 Section 6.4.1 states that within a programme there 'may be multiple project boards, a single body directing several projects, or a combination of the two' — it does not require a single board when a shared executive exists. Action 2 is wrong because concentrating sustainability accountability solely in Project Assurance is inconsistent with PRINCE2's guidance that the Project Executive is responsible for ensuring sustainability alignment (Section 6.4.4) and that accountability should be distributed through role descriptions across the team. Option C is incorrect. While Action 1 is correct, Action 2 is wrong for the same reason stated in Option B. PRINCE2 Section 6.4.4 places primary accountability for sustainability with the Project Executive and requires it to be embedded across the project ecosystem, not concentrated in a single assurance role. Option D is incorrect. Action 1 is wrong because PRINCE2 presents a single directing body as one option among several, not a requirement. Action 2 is correct but cannot redeem the option because Action 1 misrepresents PRINCE2 guidance.

topic: Plans - Product-Based Planning, Work Packages & Dependencies
question: During Stage 2 (design) of the LouisShopping project, the Project Manager is creating the product breakdown structure and product flow diagram for Stage 3. The revised public transport routes and park-and-ride scheme — both managed by a separate Louistown City Council transport project — are essential for the shopping centre to open. The Principal Electrical Building Services Engineer has also advised that the underground parking utilities cannot be installed until the structural shell of the mall is complete. Which statement BEST describes how these two dependencies should be treated in the Stage 3 plan?

options.A: Both dependencies should be treated as internal dependencies because they involve products required by the LouisShopping project; they should be documented in the product flow diagram and the associated work package descriptions.
options.B: The revised public transport routes and park-and-ride scheme are external dependencies because they are outside the scope of the LouisShopping project and are not controlled by the project team; the underground parking utilities dependency on the structural shell is an internal dependency. Both must be documented in the product flow diagram and the associated work package descriptions.
options.C: The revised public transport routes and park-and-ride scheme should be excluded from the product flow diagram because they are managed by a separate project; only products within the direct scope of Stage 3 should be shown. The underground parking utilities dependency is an internal dependency and should be included.
options.D: Both dependencies are external because the transport scheme involves a third-party council project and the structural shell will be built by BuildyBrick, who are an external supplier. They should both be escalated as issues in the project log rather than documented in the product flow diagram.

rationale: Option A is incorrect. PRINCE2 Section 7.3.2.4 defines an external dependency as one 'between a project product and a product or activity outside the scope of the project' where 'the project team does not have complete control over the dependency.' The revised public transport routes and park-and-ride scheme are managed by a separate transport project outside the scope of LouisShopping; the LouisShopping project team has no control over their delivery. They are therefore external dependencies, not internal ones. Misclassifying them as internal would misrepresent the project's risk exposure. Option B is correct. PRINCE2 Section 7.3.2.4 defines internal dependencies as those between two products within the project (where the team has control), and external dependencies as those involving products or activities outside the project's scope (where the team does not have complete control). The transport scheme is explicitly outside the LouisShopping project scope and is identified in the scenario as an external product in the product breakdown structure. The structural shell and utilities are both within Stage 3's scope, making the sequencing relationship between them an internal dependency. Both types must be documented in the product flow diagram and all dependencies shown must be documented in the associated work package descriptions, per Section 7.3.2.5. Option C is incorrect. PRINCE2 Section 7.3.2.2 explicitly states that 'the product breakdown structure can also show external products which the project is dependent on,' and the LouisShopping scenario itself illustrates this, noting the transport connections as external products in the product breakdown structure. Excluding external dependencies from the product flow diagram would create gaps in dependency management and undermine the completeness of the plan. Option D is incorrect. The structural shell built by BuildyBrick is a product within the scope of the LouisShopping project; BuildyBrick is an engaged supplier delivering a work package, so the dependency between the structural shell and the utilities installation is internal to the project. Furthermore, PRINCE2 does not direct that dependencies be raised as issues in the project log; dependencies are planning artefacts that belong in the product flow diagram and work package descriptions. Raising them as issues would be an inappropriate use of issue management.

topic: Quality Practice - Quality Register and Quality Control
question: The LouisShopping project is currently in Stage 4 (testing and inspections). The Maintenance Plumber has completed the water pump installation (Product 112). The first performance test conducted by M Silver was reviewed by S James on 17 September, with a target approval date of 25 September. The actual approval date was 28 September and the result was recorded as 'Fail'. A subsequent maintenance test was then conducted by L Kay. Reviewing the quality register in Table 8.1, which statement BEST describes what should happen next regarding the failed water pump (Product 112) performance test result?

options.A: The Project Manager should immediately raise an exception report to the Project Board because the performance test failure means the project has exceeded its quality tolerances and the board must be informed without further investigation.
options.B: Because the performance test resulted in a 'Fail', the failure must be reviewed against quality tolerances and quality specifications before deciding whether to repeat the activity or raise it as an issue through the issue management technique.
options.C: The CEO of BuildyBrick, as Senior Supplier, should approve a concession for the water pump because a supplier failing to deliver a product that meets its quality specifications automatically constitutes an off-specification that must be accepted.
options.D: The quality register entry for Product 112 should be closed and no further action is required because the subsequent maintenance test conducted by L Kay passed on 05 October, fully resolving the original performance test failure without any further consideration.

rationale: Option B is correct. PRINCE2 states that if a product fails a quality control activity such as an inspection or test, and there is an expectation that the product is likely to pass, it may be reasonable to repeat the activity. If a product cannot pass a quality control activity, the failure must be reviewed against quality tolerances and quality specifications. Exceptions to quality tolerances and off-specifications are addressed using the issue management technique. This is the correct two-step logic: first review against tolerances/specifications, then escalate via issue management if needed. Option A is incorrect because raising an exception report immediately without first reviewing the failure against quality tolerances and specifications bypasses the correct process. An exception report is raised when tolerances are forecast to be exceeded; the quality failure must first be assessed to determine whether tolerances are breached before escalation to that level. Option C is incorrect because a concession is an off-specification that is accepted by the Project Board without corrective action. It is not automatically granted whenever a supplier fails a test. The process requires the failure to be assessed, an issue report prepared if appropriate, and then a conscious decision made by the Project Board or those with delegated change authority. Option D is incorrect because the passing of the subsequent maintenance test does not automatically resolve or nullify the original performance test failure. The two tests measure different quality characteristics (performance versus maintenance), and the performance test failure still needs to be formally reviewed and addressed. The quality register records both activities separately, and lessons should also be captured.

topic: Risk Practice - Risk Responses and Risk Ownership
question: During Stage 3 of the LouisShopping project, the Heritage Director has identified a significant risk: excavation work for the underground parking garage could destabilise the foundations of adjacent historical buildings, causing structural damage. The Project Manager is considering the risk responses. The Project Executive has confirmed that the financial and reputational impact would be severe if this risk materialised. Which combination of risk responses would BEST address this threat, consistent with the approach described for the LouisShopping security risk scenario?

options.A: Accept the risk and prepare a contingent plan to fund repairs to any damaged buildings from the risk budget, as the probability of occurrence is uncertain and no cost should be committed until the risk actually materialises during excavation works.
options.B: Transfer the risk entirely to BuildyBrick by including a full indemnification clause in the construction contract, ensuring that all financial liability passes to the supplier and removing any residual risk exposure for Louistown City Council.
options.C: Reduce the risk by holding regular structural monitoring meetings with BuildyBrick's team leaders and conducting planned independent structural surveys of adjacent buildings, and transfer a portion of financial liability by including indemnification clauses in contractual arrangements with BuildyBrick.
options.D: Exploit the opportunity presented by the excavation by commissioning an archaeological survey that could generate income through heritage tourism, thereby offsetting the cost of additional structural protection measures required during construction.

rationale: Option C is correct. PRINCE2 explicitly illustrates this type of combined response approach in the LouisShopping security risk scenario, where the Project Manager uses a combination of reduce (regular meetings with BuildyBrick team leaders and planned independent site inspections) and transfer (indemnification clauses in contractual arrangements with BuildTech/BuildyBrick) responses. Applying the same logic to the structural risk, holding regular monitoring meetings and commissioning independent structural surveys reduces the probability and/or impact, while indemnification clauses in the contract transfer a portion of the financial risk to BuildyBrick. It is also well-established that multiple responses may be appropriate for significant risks. Option A is incorrect because simply accepting the risk with a contingent plan is not appropriate when the risk exposure would exceed the risk tolerance threshold, as indicated by the Project Executive's confirmation of severe financial and reputational impact. Acceptance without any proactive reduction measures would be inappropriate for a high-impact risk of this nature. Option B is incorrect because PRINCE2 explicitly states that it is important to note that some elements of risk cannot be transferred, and that transfer implies a third party accepts the risk cost but the original party retains the impact on other objectives (e.g., time delay). A full transfer claim also rarely eliminates residual risk entirely, and no single response is described as sufficient for a significant risk; a combination is more appropriate. Option D is incorrect because this option conflates a threat with an opportunity and proposes exploiting a separate potential opportunity (archaeological discovery) rather than responding to the identified structural damage threat. The scenario involves a threat to project objectives, not an opportunity. Exploiting an archaeological discovery would be a separate risk response to a separate opportunity risk entry.

topic: Quality Practice - Roles and Responsibilities
question: The LouisShopping project is preparing for Stage 4 (testing and inspections). The Project Manager needs to ensure that the quality management arrangements are correctly assigned. The Regeneration Manager has raised a concern that she is unsure who is responsible for approving the quality management approach and who should be providing people and resources to perform quality activities and accept the completed shopping centre. Which of the following statements MOST accurately describes the correct responsibilities?

options.A: The Project Manager approves the quality management approach and the CEO of BuildyBrick provides people and resources to accept the project product, as the supplier is best placed to confirm the product meets the required quality specifications.
options.B: The Project Executive approves the quality management approach and the Regeneration Manager, as Senior User, provides people and resources to perform user quality activities and is accountable for the acceptance of the project product on behalf of the users.
options.C: The Sustainability Manager, acting as Project Assurance, approves the quality management approach because they are independent of the project management team and confirm to the Project Board that the approach is compliant with business policies.
options.D: The Project Support team approves the quality management approach because they are responsible for preparing and maintaining the quality register and product register and have the most complete view of all quality control activities performed.

rationale: Option B is correct. PRINCE2 Table 8.2 clearly states that the Project Executive approves the quality management approach. The Senior User is responsible for providing the user's quality expectations and acceptance criteria, agreeing the quality management approach, providing people and resources to perform user quality activities and product acceptance, and accepting the project product while being accountable for that acceptance. In the LouisShopping scenario, the Regeneration Manager fulfils the Senior User role (concerned with practical delivery and benefits for residents), so this responsibility correctly sits with her. Option A is incorrect on both counts. The Project Manager does not approve the quality management approach; they consult with stakeholders to prepare it. The CEO of BuildyBrick, as Senior Supplier, is responsible for providing people and resources to perform supplier quality activities, not for accepting the project product. Product acceptance accountability rests with the Senior User, not the Senior Supplier. Option C is incorrect because the Sustainability Manager in a Project Assurance role does not approve the quality management approach. Project Assurance advises the Project Manager on the quality management approach and confirms to the Project Board that it is compliant with business policies, but the approval authority rests with the Project Executive. Confusing an advisory/assurance role with an approval authority role is a common error. Option D is incorrect because Project Support provides administrative support for quality controls and prepares and maintains the quality register and product register. They assist the project management team with quality procedures but have no authority to approve management products. Approval authorities are vested in the Project Board and relevant senior roles, not in administrative support functions.

topic: Issues Practice - Change Control and Off-Specifications
question: During Stage 3 of the LouisShopping project, BuildyBrick's construction team discovers that due to an unexpected underground obstruction, the water storage tank has been installed in a different location from that specified in the approved floor plan product description. The Principal Electrical Building Services Engineer has reported this to the Project Manager. The Project Manager confirms this constitutes a discrepancy against the approved project baseline. What is the CORRECT way to handle this situation according to PRINCE2?

options.A: The Project Manager should raise a request for change, justifying the modification to the floor plan product description, and identify the source of funding from the change budget, as the deviation from the approved baseline represents a proposed modification initiated by the project team.
options.B: The Project Manager should record this in the issue register as an off-specification, prepare an issue report detailing the discrepancy and its impact, and then present it to the Project Board (or those with delegated change authority) to either accept it as a concession or require corrective action.
options.C: The Project Manager should record this in the daily log as an informal issue, as it is a minor construction matter that can be resolved by BuildyBrick without involving the Project Board, and the quality register should be updated to reflect the change in location.
options.D: The Project Manager should immediately raise an exception report to the Project Board, as any deviation from an approved product description automatically constitutes a breach of tolerance that requires the Project Board to provide a revised project plan before any further construction work can continue.

rationale: Option B is correct. PRINCE2 defines an off-specification as a product that will not meet its quality specifications. In this scenario, the water storage tank installation deviates from the approved floor plan product description, which is precisely the situation described in the LouisShopping scenario ('BuildyBrick did not complete the water storage tank installation as per the floor plan... it needs to be handled formally'). PRINCE2 states that off-specification most often occurs when a supplier fails to deliver a product that fully complies with its quality specifications, and this discrepancy must be addressed through the change control procedure. The issue should be recorded in the issue register, an issue report prepared, and the Project Board (or delegated authority) must decide to either accept it as a concession or require corrective action. Option A is incorrect because a request for change is a proposal to change a baseline, typically initiated when someone wants to add or modify a feature. An off-specification occurs when the delivered product does not match the already-approved baseline. The distinction is important: the contractor has failed to meet the specification, which is an off-specification, not a proposed change. Misclassifying issue types leads to incorrect handling procedures. Option C is incorrect because PRINCE2 explicitly illustrates this exact scenario (the water tank installation) as a formal issue that must be entered in the issue register, not the daily log. The daily log is for informal issues that can be resolved easily. A deviation from an approved product description in a complex infrastructure project is explicitly treated as a formal off-specification requiring structured handling, not informal resolution. Option D is incorrect because not every off-specification automatically constitutes a breach of tolerance requiring an exception report. The correct process is to assess the issue first through the issue management procedure, prepare an issue report, and then present it to the appropriate authority for a decision. An exception report is raised when tolerances are forecast to be exceeded; this determination comes after assessment, not automatically upon discovery of any discrepancy.

topic: Quality Practice - Quality Register and Quality Control
question: The LouisShopping project is currently in Stage 4 (testing and inspections). The water pump (Product 112) failed its performance test on 17 Sep but subsequently passed its maintenance test on 04 Oct. Reviewing the quality register in Table 8.1, which statement BEST describes the correct interpretation and next action for the Project Manager?

options.A: Because the water pump passed the maintenance test, the quality register entry for the failed performance test (Quality Activity ID 2) can be deleted to keep the register accurate and uncluttered.
options.B: The quality register correctly retains both entries; the failed performance test result must be reviewed against quality tolerances and quality specifications, and if the failure represented an off-specification, the issue management technique should have been applied.
options.C: Because the maintenance test passed, the failed performance test is now superseded; the Project Manager should simply update Quality Activity ID 2's result from 'Fail' to 'Pass' to reflect the current status of the product.
options.D: The failed performance test result means the water pump cannot be accepted under any circumstances; the Project Manager must raise an exception report to the Project Board immediately, regardless of the passed maintenance test.

rationale: Option A is incorrect because the quality register is an audit record of all quality control activities that are planned or have occurred. Deleting entries would destroy the audit trail that provides key assurance information comparing what was planned against what was actually performed. The register is a record, not a live status document. Option B is correct because PRINCE2 states the quality register merely records the quality control activity and its result. A 'fail' result is not erased by a subsequent 'pass' on a different test. The failed performance test must be reviewed against quality tolerances and quality specifications. If the failure constituted an off-specification (a product that does not meet its quality specification), it must be addressed via the issue management technique. The two entries correctly co-exist as a historical audit record. Option C is incorrect for the same reason as A. Altering a historical result corrupts the integrity of the quality register as an audit document. The failed performance test was a real event with a real outcome; the subsequent maintenance test was a separate quality activity with its own result. They measure different quality attributes and cannot be merged or overwritten. Option D is incorrect because PRINCE2 does not mandate automatic exception reporting for every product failure. If a product fails a quality control activity and there is an expectation it is likely to pass another activity, it may be reasonable to repeat or conduct a different activity, as demonstrated here. An exception report is required when a tolerance is forecast to be exceeded, not simply because one quality activity fails. The Project Manager retains management by exception discretion.

topic: Risk Practice - Risk Responses and Ownership
question: During Stage 3 (construction) of LouisShopping, the Heritage Director advises that excavation for the underground parking could cause structural damage to adjacent historical buildings. The Project Executive agrees this is a significant threat. The CEO of BuildyBrick suggests three responses: (1) commissioning independent structural surveys at regular intervals during excavation; (2) including indemnification clauses in BuildyBrick's sub-contracts with specialist excavation firms; and (3) redesigning the excavation sequence to address the most vulnerable structures first. Which combination of risk responses is MOST accurately categorised according to PRINCE2?

options.A: Response 1 = Avoid; Response 2 = Transfer; Response 3 = Reduce.
options.B: Response 1 = Reduce; Response 2 = Transfer; Response 3 = Avoid.
options.C: Response 1 = Reduce; Response 2 = Share; Response 3 = Reduce.
options.D: Response 1 = Accept; Response 2 = Transfer; Response 3 = Avoid.

rationale: Option A is incorrect because Response 1 (commissioning independent structural surveys) does not remove the risk of damage occurring; it is a monitoring and early-warning measure that reduces the probability or limits the impact by enabling earlier corrective action. It is therefore a 'reduce' response, not an 'avoid' response. Avoid means making the uncertain situation certain by removing the risk entirely, typically by removing its cause. Option B is correct. Response 1 (regular independent structural surveys) is a 'reduce' response because it takes definite action to change the probability and/or impact of the threat — surveys allow early identification and intervention, reducing the likelihood of undetected damage escalating. Response 2 (indemnification clauses) is a 'transfer' response because it passes part of the financial risk to a third party (the sub-contractors), consistent with PRINCE2's definition of transfer. Response 3 (redesigning the excavation sequence to address the most vulnerable structures first) is an 'avoid' response because changing the planned approach removes or neutralises the specific threat to those structures — making a previously uncertain situation more certain by eliminating the cause of damage to the most at-risk buildings. Option C is incorrect because Response 2 is not a 'share' response. Sharing involves multiple parties sharing risk on a pain/gain basis within a supply chain. Indemnification clauses in sub-contracts are a one-way transfer of liability to the sub-contractor, which is a classic transfer mechanism. Response 3 is also not a 'reduce' — it removes the threat to vulnerable structures entirely by restructuring the approach, which is avoidance. Option D is incorrect because Response 1 is active and deliberate, not passive. 'Accept' means taking no proactive action and simply managing the full impact if the risk occurs. Commissioning surveys is a proactive reduce response. Additionally, Response 3 changes the approach to remove the threat rather than merely reduce it, so 'avoid' is correctly applied there but incorrectly positioned in this option.

topic: Issues Practice - Change Control and Off-Specification
question: During Stage 3 of LouisShopping, BuildyBrick's Maintenance Plumber discovers that the water storage tank has been installed in a position that deviates from the approved floor plan, because an unanticipated old pipe was encountered during excavation. The deviation means the tank does not fully comply with its approved product description. The Regeneration Manager believes the deviation is minor and that the tank is still functionally adequate. Which course of action BEST reflects PRINCE2 guidance on how this situation should be handled?

options.A: Because the Regeneration Manager considers the deviation minor and the tank functionally adequate, the Project Manager can update the product description informally and continue work without raising a formal issue, provided the change is noted in the daily log.
options.B: The situation constitutes an off-specification and must be entered in the issue register; if the Project Board accepts it without corrective action, it becomes a concession and the product description must be updated to reflect the approved change to the project baseline.
options.C: Because the deviation was caused by an unforeseen external event (the old pipe) rather than BuildyBrick's failure to perform, it is categorised as an event external to the project and should be managed solely through the risk register rather than the issue register.
options.D: The Project Manager should immediately raise an exception report to the Project Board, as any product that fails to comply with its quality specifications automatically causes a stage tolerance to be exceeded.

rationale: Option A is incorrect because PRINCE2 explicitly defines an off-specification as a product that will not meet its quality specifications. This situation clearly meets that definition. The guidance states that off-specifications must be addressed through the change control procedure. Using only the daily log and making informal updates to baselined management products bypasses change control entirely, undermining the project baseline integrity and the audit trail. This is contrary to PRINCE2's issue management approach. Option B is correct. PRINCE2 defines an off-specification as a product that will not meet its quality specifications, which precisely describes this situation. The deviation must be formally captured in the issue register and an issue report should be prepared. If the Project Board accepts the deviation without requiring corrective action, the accepted off-specification is termed a concession under PRINCE2. Following approval, the project baseline — including the relevant product description — must be updated to reflect the approved change. This is consistent with the LouisShopping scenario described in the syllabus. Option C is incorrect because, while the root cause involved an external event (the old pipe), the consequence is a product that does not comply with its approved product description. This is the definition of an off-specification issue, not an external event issue. External event categorisation applies to events outside the project's control that may impact the project (e.g., a supplier going out of business), not to a product delivery failure resulting from a construction complication. The appropriate category is off-specification, handled through the issue register and change control. Option D is incorrect because PRINCE2 does not state that any quality failure automatically triggers an exception report. An exception report is required when a stage tolerance is forecast to be exceeded. An off-specification is first assessed through the issue management procedure; it may be resolved within delegated change authority or via concession without necessarily breaching stage tolerances. Automatically escalating to an exception report would be disproportionate and does not reflect the graduated response framework in PRINCE2.

topic: Quality Practice - Roles and Responsibilities
question: The Project Manager for LouisShopping is preparing the quality management approach at the end of the initiation stage. The Sustainability Manager (acting in a project assurance capacity) has raised a concern that the quality management approach does not clearly define roles and responsibilities for the quality control activities to be performed by BuildyBrick under the construction contract, nor does it distinguish between contractual acceptance by BuildyBrick's client and acceptance of the project product as a whole. Which two actions would BEST address this concern in accordance with PRINCE2?

options.A: Update the quality management approach to include BuildyBrick's quality control responsibilities and specify that contractual acceptance by the customer is the same as project-level acceptance of the project product, since both involve the same physical product.
options.B: Update the quality management approach to clearly define the roles and responsibilities for quality control and contractual acceptance, distinguishing them from acceptance of the project as a whole; and ensure that product descriptions for specialist products explicitly reference the relevant clauses of the Mall Federation construction standards agreed with BuildyBrick.
options.C: Delegate responsibility for defining quality roles and responsibilities entirely to the CEO of BuildyBrick, since BuildyBrick as Senior Supplier is responsible for agreeing the quality management approach and the quality techniques adopted in product development.
options.D: Raise a formal issue in the issue register requesting that the Project Board redefine the project's acceptance criteria, as the current approach cannot be baselined without first resolving the contractual quality responsibilities.

rationale: Option A is incorrect because PRINCE2 specifically warns that care should be taken to ensure that contractual quality and acceptance criteria do not conflict with those in the product and project product descriptions, and that acceptance of a product from a supplier may be a prerequisite to acceptance by the customer but its terms are covered under the contract, not the project initiation documentation. Treating contractual acceptance as equivalent to project-level acceptance conflates two distinct processes and could lead to the project being closed before the full project product has been accepted by the Project Board on behalf of the Senior User. Option B is correct for two reasons. First, PRINCE2's commercial context guidance explicitly states that the quality management approach must ensure roles and responsibilities for quality control and contractual acceptance are clearly distinguished from the responsibilities for acceptance of the project as a whole — directly addressing the Sustainability Manager's concern. Second, PRINCE2 states that where external quality standards apply (such as the Mall Federation construction standards agreed with BuildyBrick), these should be reflected in the product descriptions by referencing specific clauses. This ensures traceability and provides subject matter experts with clear quality specifications to work against during quality control activities in Stage 4. Option C is incorrect because, while the Senior Supplier (CEO of BuildyBrick) is responsible for agreeing the quality management approach and agreeing quality techniques for product development, the responsibility for preparing and maintaining the quality management approach belongs to the Project Manager. Project assurance (the Sustainability Manager) advises the Project Manager on the quality management approach. Delegating the definition of roles and responsibilities entirely to a supplier representative would undermine the independence of quality assurance and potentially allow a supplier to set its own acceptance criteria — a clear governance risk in PRINCE2's framework. Option D is incorrect because the concern raised by the Sustainability Manager relates to a gap in the quality management approach documentation, not a change to the approved project baseline requiring an issue report. The quality management approach is still being prepared at the end of the initiation stage and has not yet been fully baselined as part of the project initiation documentation. The appropriate response is to update the approach before it is approved, not to raise a formal issue requesting the Project Board to redefine acceptance criteria.

topic: Issues Practice - Delegation of Change Authority
question: The LouisShopping project is currently in Stage 3 (construction). The CEO of BuildyBrick has informed the Project Manager that, due to unexpected ground conditions near the historical buildings, the number of underground parking spaces cannot meet the originally specified requirement. The Project Manager is assessing how change authority should be managed for this situation. Which approach to delegating change authority is MOST appropriate for the remainder of this complex, multi-stage project?

options.A: Retain all change authority with the Project Executive to ensure every off-specification decision is reviewed at the highest level, as the project involves taxpayer funds and historical sensitivity.
options.B: Delegate change authority to multiple levels within the project management team, with parameters defined in the issue management approach, balancing efficiency and control while preserving alignment with the business justification.
options.C: Delegate full change authority to the Project Manager for all off-specifications and requests for change, since the Project Manager is closest to the construction work and best placed to make timely decisions.
options.D: Delegate change authority exclusively to the CEO of BuildyBrick for all construction-related off-specifications, as the contractor is commercially accountable and responsible for the physical works.

rationale: Option A is incorrect: retaining all change authority with the Project Executive would likely slow progress and result in the Project Executive reviewing changes that others are better placed to decide. PRINCE2 warns that too little delegation can impede project progress. Option B is correct: PRINCE2 states that for projects likely to generate many changes, the project board may delegate change authority to several levels within the project management team, with parameters specified in the issue management approach. This balances efficiency (avoiding unnecessary escalation) with control (ensuring the business justification is not diluted). Given LouisShopping's complexity, multi-stage nature, and high volume of potential construction-related changes, this tiered delegation is the most appropriate approach. Option C is incorrect: delegating full change authority to the Project Manager removes the project board's oversight entirely. PRINCE2 notes that too much delegation, particularly across too many roles, risks reducing overall benefits as alignment with business justification is diluted. The Project Manager should have delegated authority within defined parameters, not unlimited authority. Option D is incorrect: delegating change authority exclusively to the CEO of BuildyBrick is inappropriate because the Senior Supplier's role is to safeguard the integrity of the complete solution from a supplier perspective on the project board, not to hold unilateral change authority. This would remove the customer's control over changes to project baselines and undermine the project's governance structure.

topic: Issues Practice - Issue Assessment and Off-Specification Response
question: During Stage 3 of the LouisShopping project, an inspection reveals that BuildyBrick has completed the water storage tank installation but it fails to meet the approved health and safety capacity specifications. The Regeneration Manager raises this formally. The Project Manager is now assessing this off-specification. According to PRINCE2, which of the following actions represents the CORRECT sequence for the Project Manager to follow when assessing this off-specification?

options.A: Immediately escalate the off-specification to the project board via an exception report, bypassing detailed assessment, because health and safety requirements are non-negotiable and require urgent board-level direction.
options.B: Capture the off-specification in the issue register, assess its impact on the approved targets and tolerances, the project business case, and other products or work packages, then formulate resolution options and a recommendation for the appropriate decision-making authority.
options.C: Record the off-specification in the daily log as an informal issue, since the Project Manager can resolve health and safety matters directly with BuildyBrick without involving the project board, keeping administration to a minimum.
options.D: Reject the off-specification immediately without assessment, directing BuildyBrick to rectify the tank at their own cost under their contractual obligations, and record the decision in the issue register.

rationale: Option A is incorrect: while the issue is serious, PRINCE2's five-step issue management technique requires capturing and assessing the issue before recommending and escalating. Bypassing assessment would deprive the project board of the impact analysis, options, and recommendations they need to make an informed decision. An exception report is raised when stage tolerances are forecast to be breached, which requires assessment first. Option B is correct: PRINCE2's issue management technique requires the Project Manager to first capture the off-specification in the issue register, then assess its impact across the project baseline (approved targets and tolerances), the business case, and other products or work packages. The assessment should ideally provide more than one resolution option. Only after this thorough assessment should a recommendation be made to the appropriate authority. This mirrors the LouisShopping scenario described in the syllabus materials for the water storage tank. Option C is incorrect: recording a health and safety off-specification in the daily log as an informal issue is inappropriate. The daily log is for informal issues that do not have a material impact on the project. A failure to meet a health and safety capacity specification is a formal off-specification with material impact that must be registered in the issue register and handled formally. Option D is incorrect: while rejecting an off-specification and pursuing a contractual remedy is a valid response option described in PRINCE2, it should not occur immediately without assessment. The Project Manager must first assess the full impact on the business case, tolerances, and other products before recommending a course of action to the appropriate authority. An immediate unilateral rejection without assessment does not follow the PRINCE2 technique.

topic: Progress Practice - Exception Management and Escalation
question: During Stage 3 of the LouisShopping project, the Principal Electrical Building Services Engineer (Team Manager) submits a checkpoint report indicating that the electrical systems work package is forecast to exceed its agreed time tolerance by three weeks due to the discovery of outdated floor plans being used by the team. This forecast breach cannot be resolved within the work package tolerances. What is the CORRECT escalation path and associated actions according to PRINCE2?

options.A: The Team Manager raises an issue to the Project Manager. If the forecast breach will take the stage outside its tolerances, the Project Manager produces an exception report for the project board, who may then request an exception plan.
options.B: The Team Manager produces an exception report directly to the project board, bypassing the Project Manager, as the issue originated at the delivering level and the project board needs immediate visibility.
options.C: The Project Manager raises the issue in the next scheduled highlight report to the project board, as time-driven reporting is the appropriate mechanism for communicating forecast tolerance breaches at work package level.
options.D: The Team Manager resolves the issue independently by commissioning a full audit of all floor plans without informing the Project Manager, using the change budget to fund the additional work required to correct the outdated drawings.

rationale: Option A is correct: PRINCE2's exception management technique specifies that when a work package is forecast to exceed its tolerances, the Team Manager raises an issue for the attention of the Project Manager. If the issue will affect stage tolerances to the point they are forecast to be breached, the Project Manager creates an exception report for the project board detailing the situation with resolution options and a recommendation. The project board may then request an exception plan. This mirrors the LouisShopping scenario where the outdated floor plans caused a week of rework and required the Project Manager to investigate dependencies and escalate to the project board. Option B is incorrect: PRINCE2 does not provide for Team Managers to produce exception reports directly to the project board. The Team Manager's reporting line is to the Project Manager. The exception report is produced by the Project Manager and sent to the project board. Bypassing the Project Manager would undermine the project's management hierarchy and the manage by exception principle. Option C is incorrect: while forecast tolerance breaches are indeed reported in highlight reports as part of time-driven reporting, a forecast breach of stage tolerance cannot wait until the next scheduled highlight report if it requires board-level decision-making. PRINCE2 requires an exception report to be raised as an event-driven control when tolerances are forecast to be exceeded, not deferred to the next routine report. The highlight report may reference the exception but is not the primary mechanism for managing it. Option D is incorrect: the Team Manager does not have the authority to independently commission audits or use the change budget without the Project Manager's direction. The change budget is allocated by those with delegated authority to deliver authorized changes. Unilateral action by the Team Manager without informing the Project Manager would breach the agreed issue and change control procedures specified in the work package description and undermine configuration control.

topic: Progress Practice - Management Products and Reporting
question: The LouisShopping project is approaching the end of Stage 3 (construction). The Project Manager needs to provide the project board with sufficient information to make a decision on whether to proceed to Stage 4 (testing and inspections). The project has experienced several issues during Stage 3, including the water storage tank off-specification and the outdated floor plans incident. Which combination of management products should the Project Manager produce to support the project board's end of stage decision, and what is the PRIMARY purpose of each?

options.A: An exception report (to inform the project board of the tolerance breaches that occurred during Stage 3) and a checkpoint report (to summarize the overall stage performance for the project board's review prior to authorizing Stage 4).
options.B: An end stage report (to give a summary of progress to date and the overall project situation, providing sufficient information for the project board to decide on next steps) and a lessons report (to share lessons from Stage 3 and trigger actions to embed learning, which may be included within or alongside the end stage report).
options.C: A highlight report (to provide the project board with a summary of Stage 3 status at the stage boundary) and an issue report (to formally document all off-specifications and changes that occurred during Stage 3 for the project board's retrospective review).
options.D: An end project report (to review how the project has performed against the project initiation documentation) and an exception report (to seek direction from the project board on whether to continue to Stage 4 given the issues encountered during Stage 3).

rationale: Option A is incorrect: an exception report is an event-driven report produced when tolerances are forecast to be exceeded and direction is needed from the project board. It is not a retrospective summary document used at stage boundaries. A checkpoint report is produced by the Team Manager to the Project Manager to report on work package status; it is not produced by the Project Manager for the project board and is not the appropriate product for an end of stage decision. Option B is correct: PRINCE2 specifies that the end stage report is produced by the Project Manager for the project board at the end of a stage, providing a summary of progress to date, the overall project situation, and sufficient information for the project board to decide what to do next with the project. Its content includes a performance review of the business case, stage objectives, quality activities, products status, and lessons. A lessons report captures and shares lessons to ensure they are embedded, and PRINCE2 notes it can typically be included as part of the end stage report. Together these two products directly support the project board's decision at a stage boundary. Option C is incorrect: a highlight report is a time-driven report produced by the Project Manager to the project board at intervals during a stage to provide a summary of current stage status. It is not the primary product for end of stage decision-making. An issue report is produced to describe a specific issue's impacts and recommend a decision; it is not an appropriate summary product for all issues across a stage. The end stage report is the correct vehicle for summarizing issues at a stage boundary. Option D is incorrect: an end project report is produced at project closure to review how the project performed against the project initiation documentation. Producing it at the end of Stage 3 would be premature as the project still has Stages 4 and 5 remaining. An exception report is not required here as the purpose at this point is to report stage completion and seek authorization to proceed, not to report a current forecast tolerance breach requiring urgent direction.

topic: Issues Practice – Change Authority and Change Budget
question: The LouisShopping project is currently in Stage 3 (construction). The CEO of BuildyBrick has submitted a request for change to upgrade the specification of the shopping centre's external cladding material to a more sustainable composite, which will increase the project cost by 8% but remain within the project board's delegated authority. The Project Manager is reviewing the issue management approach to confirm the appropriate response. Which of the following statements BEST describes how delegated change authority and change budget should be applied in this situation?

options.A: The Louistown City Council must approve the request for change because any increase in project cost requires the commissioning entity to authorise additional funds before the change budget can be accessed.
options.B: The Project Manager can approve the request for change unilaterally, provided the cost increase is funded from the change budget and recorded in the daily log rather than the issue register, to reduce administrative burden.
options.C: The project board may approve the request for change using its delegated change authority, funded from the change budget, provided the decision is recorded in the issue register and the project baseline is updated to reflect the approved change.
options.D: The Senior Supplier representing BuildyBrick should approve the request for change because the change relates to construction materials and is therefore solely within the supplier's commercial remit.

rationale: Option A is incorrect. The scenario states the cost increase remains within the project board's delegated authority. The project board is the authority for approving changes within its delegated limits; escalation to Louistown City Council (the business layer) is only required when project-level tolerances are forecast to be exceeded. Option B is incorrect. The Project Manager does not have unilateral authority to approve changes that have a material impact on cost; that authority rests with whomever it has been delegated to in the issue management approach. Furthermore, a formal request for change must be recorded in the issue register, not merely the daily log – the daily log is for informal issues and events that do not have a material impact. Option C is correct. PRINCE2 states that the project board is the ultimate authority for reviewing and approving requests for change and may delegate that authority. Where the change falls within delegated authority and can be funded from the change budget, the project board (or the role to whom authority is delegated) can approve it. The approval must be recorded in the issue register and the project baseline updated accordingly, ensuring traceability. Option D is incorrect. The Senior Supplier role (CEO of BuildyBrick) is a member of the project board and contributes to decisions with a particular focus on safeguarding the integrity of the complete solution, but they do not hold unilateral change authority. Change authority is formally delegated by the project board as described in the issue management approach, not assumed by any single board member.

topic: Issues Practice – Off-Specification and Resolution Options
question: During Stage 3 of the LouisShopping project, an inspection reveals that BuildyBrick has installed underground parking that provides only 180 spaces instead of the 200 spaces specified in the project product description. The shortfall cannot be rectified within the current design footprint without significant structural rework. The Project Manager must assess the issue and recommend a resolution to the project board. Which of the following actions BEST represents the correct application of the PRINCE2 issues practice for this scenario?

options.A: The Project Manager should classify this as a request for change because BuildyBrick is proposing to alter the project scope, record it in the issue register, assess the impact on the business case and project tolerances, and present options including acceptance as a concession, structural rework funded via the change budget, or pursuit of a contractual remedy.
options.B: The Project Manager should classify this as an off-specification, record it in the issue register, assess the impact on the project baseline and business case, and recommend options to the project board including accepting the shortfall as a concession with an updated baseline, undertaking structural rework funded via the change budget or a contractual remedy, or requesting an exception plan.
options.C: The Project Manager should classify this as a problem/concern, record it in the daily log because it is a construction defect that can be resolved operationally by BuildyBrick, and advise the CEO of BuildyBrick to remedy the shortfall within the existing work package tolerances without involving the project board.
options.D: The Project Manager should classify this as an off-specification and immediately escalate it to Louistown City Council for a decision, bypassing the project board, because the parking provision is a statutory requirement linked to Louistown's planning approval and therefore falls under the authority of the commissioning body.

rationale: Option A is incorrect. A request for change is raised when a stakeholder proposes a voluntary modification to an approved baseline. In this case, BuildyBrick has failed to deliver to the specification already agreed – this is not a proposal to change scope but a failure to meet it. PRINCE2 defines this situation specifically as an off-specification. Option B is correct. PRINCE2 defines an off-specification as a situation where a product does not or will not meet its quality specifications. The parking shortfall is a clear example. The correct procedure is to record it in the issue register, assess impacts on the business case and project tolerances (including time, cost, and scope), and present the project board with options: accepting the shortfall as a concession (updating the baseline), remedying the defect via the change budget or exception plan, or pursuing a contractual remedy against BuildyBrick as the responsible supplier. Option C is incorrect. The shortfall of 20 parking spaces has a material impact on the project product description and the project baseline; it cannot be treated as an informal issue recorded in the daily log. The daily log is reserved for issues that do not have a material impact. Furthermore, the project board must be involved in approving any concession or significant corrective action for an off-specification. Option D is incorrect. Escalation to Louistown City Council (the business layer) is only triggered when the project board determines that the issue will cause project-level tolerances to be exceeded. The project board retains authority to decide on off-specifications within its delegated limits. Bypassing the project board contradicts the PRINCE2 manage-by-exception principle and the defined escalation hierarchy.

topic: Progress Practice – Exception Management and Reporting
question: At the end of week 6 of Stage 3, the Principal Electrical Building Services Engineer (Team Manager for electrical works) submits a checkpoint report indicating that unforeseen groundwork complications have caused a forecast overrun of four weeks on the electrical work package. This will cause the overall Stage 3 plan to exceed its time tolerance of ±2 weeks, which was set by the project board. The Project Manager reviews the situation. Which sequence of actions BEST reflects the correct PRINCE2 exception management technique?

options.A: The Project Manager should immediately produce an exception report for the project board detailing the deviation, its causes, consequences, options, and a recommendation; then, if the project board requests it, cease the current stage plan, produce an exception plan, and await project board approval before resuming delivery.
options.B: The Project Manager should instruct the Principal Electrical Building Services Engineer to update the stage plan directly to absorb the four-week overrun, notify the project board in the next scheduled highlight report, and only produce an exception report if the Regeneration Manager formally requests one.
options.C: The Project Manager should raise the issue in the issue register, attempt to resolve the overrun through internal corrective actions within the work package, and only escalate to the project board if the overrun grows beyond six weeks, as minor stage tolerance breaches are within the Project Manager's discretion to manage.
options.D: The Project Manager should immediately escalate the issue to Louistown City Council, as a four-week overrun on a construction project of this strategic importance exceeds the authority of the project board and requires a business-layer decision before any exception report can be produced.

rationale: Option A is correct. PRINCE2's exception management technique specifies that when a stage plan is forecast to exceed its tolerances, the Project Manager must produce an exception report for the project board. This report details the cause of the deviation, consequences, available options, and a recommendation. The project board then has several options, one of which is to request an exception plan from the Project Manager. The Project Manager would then cease the current stage, produce the exception plan (which effectively becomes the new stage plan), and await project board approval before resuming delivery. This sequence is precisely aligned with Steps 1–6 of the PRINCE2 exception management technique. Option B is incorrect. The Project Manager does not have authority to unilaterally adjust the stage plan when tolerances are forecast to be breached; that is the project board's decision. Waiting until the next highlight report would delay the escalation and potentially worsen the impact. The production of an exception report is not contingent on a request from the Regeneration Manager. Option C is incorrect. Once it is forecast that a stage tolerance will be exceeded, the Project Manager has an obligation to raise an exception report immediately – this is the core of the manage-by-exception principle. There is no discretion to wait for a larger breach. The project board, not the Project Manager, decides corrective action when stage tolerances are forecast to be exceeded. Option D is incorrect. Escalation to Louistown City Council (the business layer) is only required when the project board determines that project-level tolerances (not merely stage tolerances) are forecast to be exceeded. A stage tolerance breach is escalated to the project board, not to the commissioning entity. Bypassing the project board would violate the defined management levels and tolerance hierarchy.

topic: Progress Practice – Tolerances, Reporting, and Management Products
question: The project board for LouisShopping is preparing to authorize Stage 4 (Testing and Inspections). The Portfolio Director has reminded the Project Executive that Louistown's 'green town' status imposes specific sustainability reporting obligations on the project, and that the project board must be able to demonstrate compliance with environmental regulations at every stage. The Project Manager is updating the project initiation documentation and the relevant management products ahead of the Stage 4 authorization. Which TWO of the following actions BEST reflect how PRINCE2 requires sustainability tolerances and progress reporting to be managed in this context? Select the answer that contains BOTH correct actions.

options.A: 1. The Project Manager should define specific sustainability tolerances for Stage 4 in the stage plan, aligned with the sustainability parameters established in the business case, and ensure the Sustainability Manager (Project Assurance) is tasked with monitoring external sources of potential sustainability issues. 2. Sustainability reporting should be integrated into the regular highlight reports and checkpoint reports rather than treated as a separate, standalone activity, so that evidence of compliance is a consequence of routine progress management.
options.B: 1. The Project Manager should record sustainability targets only in the project product description (acceptance criteria) and not in the stage plan, because sustainability is a quality tolerance rather than a progress tolerance and therefore does not need to be tracked at stage level. 2. The Sustainability Manager should produce a separate standalone sustainability report for each highlight report period, independent of the project's progress management cycle, to ensure regulatory compliance is not diluted by being merged with general progress data.
options.C: 1. The Project Manager should delegate all sustainability reporting obligations to the CEO of BuildyBrick as the Senior Supplier, because sustainability impacts arise from construction activities and are therefore the supplier's commercial and operational responsibility. 2. The project board should waive stage-level sustainability tolerances for Stage 4 because testing and inspections do not involve active construction and therefore carry no material sustainability risk.
options.D: 1. Sustainability tolerances for Stage 4 need not be set in the stage plan; instead, the Project Manager should rely on the project-level sustainability tolerances in the business case and escalate directly to Louistown City Council if any breach is detected. 2. The digital and data management approach should describe how sustainability data will be collected, analysed, and reported, and the Project Executive remains accountable for sustainability reporting to the business layer throughout the project.

rationale: Option A is correct on both counts. First, PRINCE2 explicitly states that more specific stage-level sustainability tolerances may be set by the project board when authorizing a stage, and that the issue management approach should ensure someone in the project management team monitors external sources of potential sustainability issues – making the Sustainability Manager's monitoring role appropriate. Second, PRINCE2 states that sustainability reporting should not be separate from the agreed reporting requirements but rather integrated into the cyclical analysis of project data, so that evidential reporting is a consequence of progress management rather than an additional activity. Both actions align precisely with PRINCE2 guidance. Option B is incorrect on both counts. Sustainability is explicitly listed as one of the seven tolerance types in PRINCE2, and stage-level sustainability tolerances can and should be set in the stage plan. Sustainability is not classified solely as a quality tolerance. Additionally, PRINCE2 explicitly states that sustainability reporting should be integrated into regular reporting cycles rather than kept as a separate standalone activity. Option C is incorrect on both counts. While the Senior Supplier (CEO of BuildyBrick) has responsibility for sustainability reporting from the supplier perspective, PRINCE2 states that the Project Executive remains accountable for sustainability reporting to the business layer for the project as a whole – this accountability cannot be fully delegated away. Furthermore, waiving sustainability tolerances for Stage 4 based on an assumption of reduced risk contradicts the PRINCE2 requirement to set appropriate tolerances for each stage. Option D is partially correct (the Project Executive's accountability for sustainability reporting is accurate) but incorrect in its first statement. PRINCE2 specifically states that stage-level sustainability tolerances should be set when authorizing a stage, and the escalation path for a stage-level breach goes to the project board first – not directly to Louistown City Council. Direct escalation to the business layer only occurs when project-level tolerances are forecast to be exceeded. Because the first statement in Option D contradicts PRINCE2 guidance, the entire option is not the best answer.

topic: Starting Up a Project - Pre-Project Activities and Responsibilities
question: The Louistown City Council has issued a project mandate for the 'LouisShopping' shopping centre project. The Portfolio Director is questioning why a project brief needs to be created when the council has already approved a strategic business case and a high-level project plan. Which statement BEST explains the purpose of assembling the project brief in this context?

options.A: The project brief replaces the strategic business case approved by the council and provides the definitive justification for committing significant expenditure to the construction works.
options.B: The project brief ensures that the project has a commonly understood and well-defined starting point, refining the information in the project mandate to provide the project board with sufficient information to decide whether to proceed to initiation.
options.C: The project brief is only required when a project is not part of a portfolio; since LouisShopping sits within the LOB portfolio, the Portfolio Director's view is correct and the project brief can be omitted.
options.D: The project brief is assembled primarily to document the appointment of BuildyBrick as the main contractor and to confirm the traditional delivery method required by the council.

rationale: Option B is correct. PRINCE2 states that the project board must be provided with sufficient information to make the decision to initiate the project, and the project brief is prepared for this purpose. It refines the information in the project mandate and provides a commonly understood and well-defined starting point. Even when a strategic business case already exists (as issued by the council), the project brief consolidates scope, approach, constraints, and the outline business case into a form usable by the project board. Option A is incorrect because the project brief does not replace the strategic business case; it builds upon it and the outline business case is a component within the project brief, not a substitute for it. Option C is incorrect because PRINCE2 acknowledges that when a project is part of a programme or portfolio, the programme/portfolio may provide much of the content of the project brief, but the project manager should still validate what is provided and recommend modifications; the project brief is not omitted entirely. Option D is incorrect because whilst the project brief does document the project approach (which would reference the traditional delivery method) and may reference appointed parties, its primary purpose is to give the project board enough information to decide whether to initiate the project, not merely to record contractual appointments.

topic: Directing a Project - Authorize a Stage or Exception Plan
question: The LouisShopping project is nearing the end of Stage 2 (design). The Project Manager has submitted the end stage report and the Stage 3 plan covering site preparation, utilities, and construction. The CEO of BuildyBrick has proposed that, given the complexity and multi-year span of Stage 3, the Project Executive alone should review and approve the stage plan at a one-to-one meeting with the Project Manager, without involving the Business Opportunities Director or the Regeneration Manager. Is this approach consistent with PRINCE2?

options.A: Yes, because the Project Executive has overall accountability for the project and has the authority to make decisions unilaterally on behalf of the project board, provided the Project Manager is informed.
options.B: Yes, because the CEO of BuildyBrick, as the Senior Supplier representative, has the most relevant interest in Stage 3 construction activities, so their agreement is the only additional input needed.
options.C: No, because authorizing a stage or exception plan is a project board activity, and the RACI chart shows the Project Executive is Accountable/Responsible but the Business Opportunities Director (Senior User) and CEO of BuildyBrick (Senior Supplier) should be Consulted, ensuring all three stakeholder interests are represented.
options.D: No, because stage authorization decisions must always be taken by the Louistown City Council at the corporate/portfolio level, since Stage 3 spans more than one financial year and exceeds normal project board authority.

rationale: Option C is correct. PRINCE2's RACI chart for directing a project shows that for 'Authorize a stage or exception plan', the Project Executive is Accountable/Responsible, but the Senior User and Senior Supplier should be Consulted. The project board as a whole must authorize each stage, reviewing the end stage report and approving the next stage plan; it is not an activity for the Project Executive alone.
