All three stakeholder interests (business, user, supplier) must be engaged. Option A is incorrect because whilst the Project Executive holds accountability, PRINCE2 requires the project board to provide unified direction. Excluding the Senior User and Senior Supplier risks contradictory requirements and undermines the governance structure. The project board manages by exception collectively, and decisions at stage boundaries involve all members. Option B is incorrect because while the CEO of BuildyBrick as Senior Supplier has a significant interest in the construction stage, PRINCE2 does not give any single board member unilateral authority to approve stage plans. All three interests must be represented. The Business Opportunities Director and Regeneration Manager (Senior Users) are also critical given the impact on residents and the commercial goals. Option D is incorrect because stage authorization is explicitly the project board's responsibility within the directing a project process. The Louistown City Council sits in the business/corporate layer and is kept Informed, not the authorizing body for individual stages. Only deviations from the project plan (not stage plans) require business layer approval.

topic: Initiating a Project - Management Approaches
question: During the initiation stage of LouisShopping, the Project Manager is working through the nine management approaches. The Sustainability Manager has advised that, given Louistown's recent 'green town' status, the sustainability management approach should be the first approach drafted so that its requirements cascade into all others. Is this consistent with PRINCE2 guidance on the order in which management approaches should be agreed?

options.A: Yes, because the sustainability management approach underpins the project's compliance with Louistown's green town status and must be baselined first to ensure all other approaches reference it correctly.
options.B: No, because PRINCE2 recommends that the benefits management approach should always be completed first, as it is derived directly from the business case approved by the project board.
options.C: No, because PRINCE2 recommends that the communication management approach should be completed last, as it needs to include the communications required by all the other approaches; the sustainability management approach has no prescribed first-position requirement.
options.D: No, because PRINCE2 requires the quality management approach to be completed first, since users' quality expectations and acceptance criteria must be defined before any other management approach can be drafted.

rationale: Option C is correct. PRINCE2 explicitly states that the activities to establish the management approaches may be executed in parallel, but it is recommended that the communication management approach is completed last, as it will need to include the communications required by the other approaches. There is no PRINCE2 directive that places the sustainability management approach first; the Sustainability Manager's suggestion, while understandable from a context perspective, is not aligned with the method's guidance. Option A is incorrect because whilst the sustainability requirements arising from Louistown's green town status are important and will influence the project, PRINCE2 does not prescribe that the sustainability management approach must be drafted first. The method only specifies that the communication management approach should be last. Option B is incorrect because PRINCE2 does not state that the benefits management approach must be the first to be completed. The benefits management approach is one of the nine approaches and may be developed in parallel with others; only the communication management approach has a specific recommended sequencing (last). Option D is incorrect because PRINCE2 does not prescribe that the quality management approach must be completed first among the management approaches. Users' quality expectations are already captured in the project product description (from starting up a project) and the quality management approach builds on this, but there is no rule mandating it as the first approach to be agreed.

topic: Starting Up a Project - RACI and Roles
question: At the very beginning of the LouisShopping project, before the Project Manager has been formally appointed, the Regeneration Manager is questioning who should be creating the outline business case and the project product description. Referencing the RACI chart for the starting up a project process, which of the following statements is MOST accurate?

options.A: The outline business case is created by the Project Manager (Responsible) under the accountability of the Project Executive; if no Project Manager is yet appointed, the Project Executive or someone appointed by them may perform this work, maintaining the single point of accountability.
options.B: The outline business case is the sole responsibility of the Business Opportunities Director as the Senior User, since they represent those who will benefit from the project and must confirm the expected outcomes.
options.C: The project product description is the sole responsibility of the CEO of BuildyBrick as Senior Supplier, since only the supplier has the technical expertise to define what will be delivered.
options.D: Both the outline business case and project product description must wait until the full project management team is appointed, as PRINCE2 prohibits the creation of any management products before all roles are filled.

rationale: Option A is correct. The RACI chart for starting up a project shows that the Project Manager is Responsible and the Project Executive is Accountable for preparing the outline business case. PRINCE2 further states that if a project manager has not been appointed until later in the process, the required management products may be created by the project executive or anyone appointed by them, while maintaining the single point of accountability for each role's duty. Option B is incorrect because the RACI chart shows the Senior User (here, the Business Opportunities Director and/or Regeneration Manager) is Consulted (when appointed) during the preparation of the outline business case, not Responsible or Accountable. The Project Executive holds accountability and the Project Manager holds responsibility for this activity. Option C is incorrect because the project product description is created during the 'Prepare the outline business case' activity, where the Project Manager is Responsible (consulting the Senior User, Senior Supplier, and Project Executive). The CEO of BuildyBrick as Senior Supplier is Consulted, not solely responsible. Furthermore, PRINCE2 is clear that defining what the project is to deliver is done by the Project Manager in consultation with stakeholders, not unilaterally by the supplier. Option D is incorrect because PRINCE2 explicitly states that the aim of starting up a project is to do the minimum necessary to decide whether it is worthwhile to initiate the project. Work begins as soon as the project executive and project manager are appointed (the first activity). The process does not require all roles to be filled before any management products can be created; in fact, it is designed to enable the project to proceed before the full team is assembled.

topic: Directing a Project - Give Ongoing Direction and Exception Handling
question: During Stage 3 of LouisShopping, the Heritage Director raises a significant issue: excavation for the underground car park has revealed previously unknown historical artefacts, threatening to delay the construction timeline by 12 weeks and breach the stage tolerance. The Project Manager has produced an exception report. According to PRINCE2, what is the CORRECT sequence of events that should follow?

options.A: The Project Manager should immediately produce an exception plan without waiting for the project board's direction, then submit it to the project board for retrospective approval to avoid further delay to the construction programme.
options.B: The Project Executive alone should review the exception report, make a unilateral decision on how to proceed, and instruct the Project Manager accordingly, since the project board manages by exception and other members need not be involved in mid-stage decisions.
options.C: The project board reviews the exception report via the 'give ongoing direction' activity; if they decide an exception plan is required, they request the Project Manager to produce one via the managing a stage boundary process; the exception plan then replaces the current stage plan upon approval.
options.D: The Louistown City Council (corporate layer) must directly intervene because the historical artefacts represent an unforeseen constraint not in the original project mandate, meaning the project board has no authority to respond until the council revises the mandate.

rationale: Option C is correct. PRINCE2 states that in response to an exception report, the project board (within the 'give ongoing direction' activity) seeks advice if needed and makes a decision. If an exception has occurred during the stage, the project board may request that the Project Manager produces an exception plan. Only exceptions to stage plans need to be escalated for approval by the project board. The exception plan is then produced through the managing a stage boundary process and, if approved, replaces the plan that is in exception as the new baselined plan. Option A is incorrect because the Project Manager must not produce an exception plan without first receiving direction from the project board. PRINCE2 is clear that the exception plan is only produced after the project board requests it via the 'give ongoing direction' activity. Producing it in advance and seeking retrospective approval bypasses the governance structure and undermines the project board's control. Option B is incorrect because whilst the Project Executive is Accountable/Responsible for the 'give ongoing direction' activity with respect to business-related matters, the Senior User (for user-related matters) and Senior Supplier (for supplier-related matters) are also Responsible within this activity. A discovery of historical artefacts would affect user interests (the Regeneration Manager) and supplier interests (CEO of BuildyBrick). PRINCE2 requires the project board to provide unified direction; the Project Executive cannot act entirely unilaterally on matters affecting all three interests. Option D is incorrect because the project board is specifically empowered to handle in-stage exceptions through the 'give ongoing direction' activity without needing the corporate layer to intervene. The Louistown City Council (business layer) is kept Informed of project board activities. Only exceptions to the project plan (not stage plan) require business layer approval. An in-stage breach of tolerance is precisely the scenario the project board's exception handling process is designed to address.

topic: Starting Up a Project - Roles and Responsibilities
question: The Louistown City Council has just issued a project mandate for the LouisShopping project. The Portfolio Director is reviewing who should take the first actions. According to PRINCE2 7th Edition, which of the following BEST describes the correct sequence of appointments during the 'Starting Up a Project' process for the LOB portfolio?

options.A: The Portfolio Director appoints the Project Executive, then the Project Executive and Project Manager jointly appoint the project management team, then the Project Manager creates the daily log.
options.B: The Louistown City Council reviews the project mandate, appoints the Project Executive, the Project Executive then appoints the Project Manager, and the Project Manager creates the daily log.
options.C: The Project Manager is appointed first by the Louistown City Council, creates the daily log, and then the Project Executive is identified from the project mandate.
options.D: The Portfolio Director appoints both the Project Executive and Project Manager simultaneously, and the project management team is then appointed by the Project Manager alone.

rationale: Option B is correct. PRINCE2 states that the business (in this case Louistown City Council, representing the business layer) reviews the project mandate, identifies and appoints the Project Executive, the Project Executive then appoints the Project Manager, and the Project Manager creates the daily log as a repository for project information. This is the defined sequence in the 'Appoint the project executive and project manager' activity. Option A is incorrect because the Portfolio Director does not unilaterally appoint the Project Executive; the business layer (Louistown City Council) holds this accountability, and the project management team is not jointly appointed by the Project Executive and Project Manager in that combined way — the Project Executive in consultation with others drives team appointments. Option C is incorrect because the Project Manager is not the first appointment; the Project Executive must be appointed first, as they are the prerequisite decision-maker with authority who then appoints the Project Manager. Option D is incorrect because PRINCE2 requires these appointments to be sequential rather than simultaneous, and the project management team appointment involves the Project Executive in consultation with the Senior User and Project Manager, not the Project Manager alone.

topic: Directing a Project - Authorize a Stage or Exception Plan
question: The LouisShopping project is approaching the end of Stage 2 (design). The Project Manager has submitted a next stage request to the Project Board. During review, the CEO of BuildyBrick raises a concern that the Stage 3 plan, which covers site preparation and construction, may breach cost tolerances due to newly discovered underground archaeological findings near the proposed parking area. According to PRINCE2 7th Edition, which of the following actions MOST CORRECTLY describes what should happen next within the 'Directing a Project' process?

options.A: The Project Board should instruct the Project Manager to produce an exception plan, replacing the Stage 3 plan, before authorizing Stage 3 to proceed.
options.B: The Project Board should review the end stage report and the Stage 3 plan, assess the updated business case and project plan, and either approve Stage 3 with revised tolerances, request revisions, or instruct premature closure.
options.C: The CEO of BuildyBrick, as Senior Supplier, should independently revise the Stage 3 plan and resubmit it to the Project Manager for approval before the Project Board is involved.
options.D: The Project Manager should resolve the tolerance concern independently by adjusting the project plan and only inform the Project Board via the next highlight report.

rationale: Option B is correct. The 'Authorize a stage or exception plan' activity in Directing a Project requires the Project Board to review and approve the end stage report, review the stage plan for the next stage (Stage 3), check the updated business case and project plan for continued viability, and then make a decision to approve, request revision, or instruct premature closure. The concern about cost is assessed at this decision point. Option A is incorrect because an exception plan is produced when a current approved plan is forecast to go beyond its agreed tolerances during execution — not at a stage boundary review where the issue is raised before Stage 3 has even been authorized. The correct mechanism at a stage boundary is the 'authorize a stage or exception plan' activity, not an exception plan request at this juncture. Option C is incorrect because the CEO of BuildyBrick, representing supplier interests, does not have the authority to independently revise and resubmit stage plans; plan revision and approval authority sits with the Project Manager to draft and the Project Board to approve. Option D is incorrect because the concern involves potential tolerance breach at the project level, which requires escalation to the Project Board; the Project Manager cannot unilaterally adjust the project plan without Project Board approval, and a highlight report is not the appropriate vehicle for seeking such decisions.

topic: Initiating a Project - Management Approaches
question: During the initiation stage of LouisShopping, the Project Manager is defining the nine management approaches. Louistown has recently received 'green town' status, imposing sustainability requirements on the development. The Sustainability Manager has advised that a specific sustainability management approach must be created. According to PRINCE2 7th Edition, which statement BEST describes the correct relationship between the sustainability management approach and the other management approaches during the 'Initiating a Project' process?

options.A: The sustainability management approach should be completed first, as it will define the constraints and requirements that all other management approaches must comply with, including the communication management approach.
options.B: The sustainability management approach is one of nine management approaches created during initiating a project; the activities to establish all approaches may run in parallel, but the communication management approach should be completed last as it incorporates communications required by all other approaches.
options.C: The sustainability management approach is optional and only required when a specific regulatory body mandates it; for LouisShopping, it should be incorporated into the risk management approach rather than created separately.
options.D: The communication management approach should be produced first to establish how the sustainability requirements will be communicated, after which the sustainability management approach can be drafted.

rationale: Option B is correct. PRINCE2 7th Edition explicitly lists the sustainability management approach as one of the nine management approaches produced during 'Initiating a Project.' The manual states that activities to establish approaches may run in parallel but recommends that the communication management approach is completed last, as it needs to incorporate the communications required by all the other approaches — including the sustainability management approach. For LouisShopping, the 'green town' status makes this approach particularly important. Option A is incorrect because PRINCE2 does not specify that the sustainability management approach must be completed first. While sustainability constraints will influence other approaches, the recommended sequence is to complete the communication management approach last, not the sustainability management approach first. Option C is incorrect because PRINCE2 7th Edition explicitly lists the sustainability management approach as a distinct, standalone management approach — not optional and not to be subsumed into the risk management approach. The 'green town' status further reinforces its necessity. Option D is incorrect because the communication management approach is specifically recommended to be completed last, not first; it must incorporate the communication needs arising from all other approaches, meaning those approaches need to be defined before the communication approach can be finalized.

topic: Starting Up a Project - Outline Business Case and Project Brief
question: The Louistown City Council has issued a project mandate for LouisShopping. The Business Opportunities Director is keen to ensure that the outline business case properly reflects the commercial opportunity for Louistown. According to PRINCE2 7th Edition, which of the following statements MOST ACCURATELY describes accountability and responsibility for the outline business case and project brief during 'Starting Up a Project'?

options.A: The Project Manager is accountable for the outline business case and the project brief, with the Project Executive consulted on both; the Business Opportunities Director has no formal PRINCE2 role at this stage.
options.B: The Project Executive is accountable for the outline business case and assembling the project brief, with the Project Manager responsible for executing both activities; the Senior User is consulted if appointed.
options.C: The Business Opportunities Director, as the primary user representative, is accountable for the outline business case, while the Project Manager is responsible for the project brief, with the Project Executive consulted.
options.D: The Project Manager is accountable for both the outline business case and the project brief, with project assurance responsible for reviewing and approving both before submission to the Project Board.

rationale: Option B is correct. According to the RACI chart for 'Starting Up a Project,' the Project Executive is accountable (A) for the 'Prepare the outline business case' activity, with the Project Manager responsible (R) for executing it. The Senior User is consulted (C) if and when appointed. Similarly, for 'Assemble the project brief,' the Project Executive is accountable and the Project Manager is responsible. The Business Opportunities Director, in the role of Senior User, is consulted. Option A is incorrect because it reverses the accountability and responsibility assignments. The Project Manager is responsible for executing the work, while the Project Executive holds accountability — not the other way around. Additionally, the Business Opportunities Director, as Senior User, does have a consultative role. Option C is incorrect because the Business Opportunities Director (Senior User) is consulted, not accountable. Accountability for the outline business case sits with the Project Executive, and the Project Executive is not merely consulted — they are accountable and share responsibility for developing the business case. Option D is incorrect because the Project Manager is responsible (not accountable) for both activities. Project assurance has a consultative role (C) and does not approve these products — that is the Project Board's responsibility. Approval authority for these documents ultimately rests with the Project Board through the 'Directing a Project' process.

topic: Directing a Project - Give Ongoing Direction
question: During Stage 3 of LouisShopping (site preparation and construction), the Mayor of Louistown contacts the Project Executive directly, demanding that the project adopt a specific new sustainability framework that has not been included in the approved project initiation documentation. This would require significant replanning. According to PRINCE2 7th Edition, which of the following responses BEST reflects the correct application of the 'Give Ongoing Direction' activity within the 'Directing a Project' process?

options.A: The Project Executive should immediately instruct the Project Manager to revise the management approaches and project plan to incorporate the new framework, as the Mayor represents the business layer whose instructions take precedence over approved plans.
options.B: The Project Board should treat the Mayor's request as a potential change to the project mandate from the business layer, and decide either to treat it as a request for change (asking the Project Manager to replan) or to trigger a premature close and start a new project, communicating this to the Project Manager.
options.C: The Project Manager should log the Mayor's request as an issue, assess the impact, and present options to the Project Board via the next highlight report, without escalating until the report is due.
options.D: The Project Executive should reject the Mayor's request outright, as the project initiation documentation has been baselined and no changes are permissible once a stage is underway.

rationale: Option B is correct. PRINCE2 7th Edition states that in the 'Give Ongoing Direction' activity, it is possible for the business to revise the project mandate in response to external events. When this occurs, the Project Board has two primary options: treat it as a request for change and ask the Project Manager to replan the stage or project, or stop and trigger a premature close before starting a new project to meet the changed mandate. The Mayor's demand represents a change instruction from the business layer (external to the project), and this is precisely the type of situation the 'Give Ongoing Direction' activity addresses. Option A is incorrect because the Project Executive cannot simply instruct the Project Manager to revise approved plans without going through the proper change control process. Instructions from the business layer that alter the approved project initiation documentation require a formal decision by the Project Board, not a unilateral instruction. Option C is incorrect because while logging an issue is appropriate, waiting until the next highlight report is insufficient when the change has significant replanning implications. The Project Manager should escalate this to the Project Board promptly, and the resolution mechanism is not the highlight report but rather the Project Board's 'Give Ongoing Direction' decision-making process. Option D is incorrect because PRINCE2 does not permit the Project Executive to unilaterally reject business-layer instructions. The business layer has the authority to revise the project mandate. The correct response is for the Project Board to assess the impact and decide how to proceed — change or premature closure — not an outright rejection by the Project Executive alone.

topic: Initiating a Project – RACI and Tailoring
question:
The Project Manager for the LouisShopping project is preparing for the initiation stage. Regarding the activity 'Agree tailoring requirements', which of the following statements BEST reflects the correct application of PRINCE2 7th Edition responsibilities?

Additional Information: The CEO of BuildyBrick has requested that tailoring decisions be made jointly and equally with the Louistown City Council Project Executive, arguing that as the main contractor they have equal stake in how the project is managed.

options.A: The CEO of BuildyBrick's request is consistent with PRINCE2, as the Senior Supplier is Accountable for agreeing tailoring requirements alongside the Project Executive.
options.B: The CEO of BuildyBrick's request is inconsistent with PRINCE2, as the Project Executive is solely Accountable for agreeing tailoring requirements, with the Senior Supplier only Consulted.
options.C: The CEO of BuildyBrick's request is inconsistent with PRINCE2, as the Project Manager is Accountable for agreeing tailoring requirements, not the Project Executive.
options.D: The CEO of BuildyBrick's request is consistent with PRINCE2, as both the Project Executive and Senior Supplier share Accountability for all initiation activities equally.

rationale: Option B is correct. Per the RACI chart for initiating a project (Table 15.2), the Project Executive (representing Louistown City Council) is Accountable (A) for 'Agree tailoring requirements', while the Senior Supplier (CEO of BuildyBrick) is only Consulted (C). The Project Manager holds Responsible (R). The CEO of BuildyBrick's request to share equal accountability is therefore inconsistent with PRINCE2. Option A is incorrect because the RACI table clearly shows the Senior Supplier as Consulted (C), not Accountable (A), for this activity; accountability rests solely with the Project Executive. Option C is incorrect because although the Project Manager is Responsible (R) for agreeing tailoring requirements – meaning they do the work – the Project Executive retains Accountability (A), which is a distinct and higher level of ownership. Option D is incorrect because PRINCE2 never assigns shared or equal Accountability between two roles for the same activity; the Project Executive holds sole Accountability for initiation activities such as agreeing tailoring, while the Senior Supplier's role is advisory through Consultation.

topic: Controlling a Stage – Escalating Issues and Risks
question:
During Stage 3 (construction) of the LouisShopping project, the Principal Electrical Building Services Engineer reports that a significant delay in the supply of specialist cabling means the work package for electrical installation is now forecast to exceed its agreed cost tolerance by 18%. The Project Manager has assessed that no corrective action within their authority can prevent the stage cost tolerance being breached.

Which of the following actions BEST describes what the Project Manager should do FIRST, in accordance with PRINCE2 7th Edition?

options.A: Immediately produce a full exception report and submit it to the Project Board, containing the situation, options for recovery, and a recommendation, without any prior notification.
options.B: Update the issue register, then send an early notification to the Project Board alerting them to the forecast exception situation, and subsequently produce the exception report with supporting detail.
options.C: Instruct the Principal Electrical Building Services Engineer to take corrective action by sourcing an alternative cabling supplier and update the stage plan, as this is within the Project Manager's authority.
options.D: Raise the issue in the highlight report at the next scheduled reporting period so the Project Board can be formally informed at the agreed reporting frequency.

rationale: Option B is correct. PRINCE2 7th Edition (Section 16.4.7) explicitly recommends that the Project Manager execute the escalation activity in two steps: first, an early notification to the Project Board to prepare them for the exception situation, and then follow up with the full exception report containing the supporting analysis, options, and recommendation. This approach gives the Project Board maximum time to consider their response. Option A is incorrect because while producing an exception report is necessary, PRINCE2 recommends alerting the Project Board as early as possible before the full report is ready, rather than waiting until the complete document is assembled. Simply submitting the full report without prior notification removes valuable reaction time for the Project Board. Option C is incorrect because the Project Manager has already determined that no corrective action within their authority can prevent the tolerance breach. Instructing the team manager to source an alternative supplier may be a valid option to present to the Project Board, but the Project Manager cannot unilaterally authorise this course of action once tolerances are forecast to be exceeded; the situation must be escalated. Option D is incorrect because waiting until the next highlight report would be a serious governance failure. Highlight reports are for routine progress updates within tolerance; a forecast tolerance breach requires immediate escalation via the exception reporting mechanism, not deferral to a scheduled report.

topic: Managing a Stage Boundary – Update the Business Case
question:
The LouisShopping project is approaching the end of Stage 3 (construction). During this stage, new surveys revealed that the underground car park excavation has caused minor structural stress to two adjacent historical buildings, resulting in unplanned remediation costs of £450,000. The Regeneration Manager has also advised that the town's 'green town' sustainability requirements have been tightened, potentially increasing material costs further.

According to PRINCE2 7th Edition, which of the following BEST describes the Project Manager's responsibilities when updating the business case at the end of Stage 3?

options.A: The Project Manager should update the business case independently, as they are Accountable for this activity at stage boundaries, and present the revised version to the Project Board for noting only.
options.B: The Project Manager should consult with the Project Executive when reviewing and updating the business case, assess the aggregated risk exposure including the new structural and sustainability risks, and revise the business case and benefits management approach ready for the Project Board's approval.
options.C: The Project Manager should refer the business case update solely to the Senior User (Regeneration Manager), as they are best placed to assess the impact of sustainability and disruption costs on project viability.
options.D: The Project Manager should defer updating the business case until Stage 4, as mid-project business case updates are only triggered by exception reports, not by end-of-stage reviews.

rationale: Option B is correct. PRINCE2 7th Edition (Section 18.4.4) explicitly states that the Project Executive is accountable for the business case, and the Project Manager should consult with them when reviewing and updating it. The recommended actions also include assessing the aggregated risk exposure (including new risks such as the structural damage and increased sustainability costs) to confirm it remains within risk tolerances, updating the benefits management approach, and presenting the revised business case to the Project Board for approval at the stage boundary. Option A is incorrect because the Project Manager is Responsible (R) for updating the business case, not Accountable (A). The RACI table (Table 18.2) shows the Project Executive as Accountable (A) for 'Update the business case'. Furthermore, the business case is presented for the Project Board's approval, not merely for noting. Option C is incorrect because while the Regeneration Manager (Senior User) is Consulted (C) on this activity and provides valuable input regarding sustainability and disruption, the responsibility for the update lies with the Project Manager in consultation with the Project Executive. Delegating the task solely to the Senior User would be inconsistent with PRINCE2's role assignments. Option D is incorrect because updating the business case is a mandatory activity within the 'managing a stage boundary' process (Section 18.4.4), which occurs at every stage end. It is not limited to exception situations; reviewing and updating the business case at each stage boundary is fundamental to PRINCE2's principle of continued business justification.

topic: Managing Product Delivery – Accepting and Executing a Work Package
question:
During Stage 2 (design) of the LouisShopping project, the Project Manager has authorised a work package to the Maintenance Plumber (Team Manager) covering the design and specification of all underground utilities. The Maintenance Plumber has reviewed the work package description and believes the timescales are achievable but has identified that the tolerances set for cost are insufficient given the uncertainty around ground conditions near the historical buildings.

In accordance with PRINCE2 7th Edition, which of the following actions should the Maintenance Plumber take NEXT?

options.A: Accept the work package as authorised, begin executing the work, and raise the cost tolerance concern as an issue directly with the Project Board, bypassing the Project Manager.
options.B: Refuse to accept the work package until the Project Manager revises the cost tolerances to a level the Maintenance Plumber considers sufficient.
options.C: Produce a team plan demonstrating the constraint, raise the cost tolerance concern as an issue with the Project Manager, and seek agreement before formally accepting the work package.
options.D: Accept the work package and proceed, recording the cost tolerance concern in the lessons log, as cost tolerance issues can only be escalated once the work package is underway and actual overspend is confirmed.

rationale: Option C is correct. PRINCE2 7th Edition (Section 17.4.1) states that there should be an agreement between the Project Manager and the Team Manager as to what is to be delivered before a work package is allocated. The recommended actions for accepting a work package include producing a team plan to show which products can be completed within the given constraints, and consulting appropriately before agreeing to deliver the work package. If the Maintenance Plumber believes the cost tolerances are insufficient, this must be discussed and resolved with the Project Manager before acceptance, not after. Producing the team plan evidences the concern and supports the conversation. Option A is incorrect because Team Managers do not escalate issues directly to the Project Board; this bypasses the defined management hierarchy. The Team Manager's escalation path is to the Project Manager (Section 17.4.2), who then decides whether to escalate further. Raising concerns with the Project Board directly would undermine the control structure PRINCE2 establishes. Option B is incorrect because refusing to accept a work package outright without engaging collaboratively with the Project Manager is not consistent with the PRINCE2 approach described in Section 17.5.1, which emphasises a collaborative relationship between the Project Manager and Team Manager. The appropriate course is to raise the concern constructively and seek resolution, not to simply refuse. Option D is incorrect because recording a known tolerance problem in the lessons log and proceeding regardless is a governance failure. Issues that could cause a work package to exceed its tolerances must be raised before execution commences (Section 17.4.2), not deferred until an actual overspend occurs. Waiting for confirmed overspend removes the opportunity for timely corrective action.

topic: Initiating a Project - Tailoring and Management Approaches
question: The LouisShopping project has just commenced Stage 2 (design). During the initiation stage, the Project Manager drafted several management approaches. The Sustainability Manager has now raised a concern that the quality management approach does not adequately reference the 'green town' sustainability requirements, and the CEO of BuildyBrick has suggested that, given the commercial relationship, the change management approach should include a formal variation order process. Which of the following statements BEST describes how these concerns should have been addressed during initiation?

options.A: The Sustainability Manager's concern should have been addressed by the Project Executive as they are accountable for agreeing tailoring requirements, while the CEO of BuildyBrick's suggestion should have been logged as an issue for Stage 2 since supplier input into management approaches is not appropriate during initiation.
options.B: Both concerns should have been captured during the 'agree management approaches' activity, where the Project Manager is responsible and the Senior Supplier (CEO of BuildyBrick) and Senior User roles (including the Sustainability Manager acting as project assurance) are consulted, ensuring sustainability and commercial requirements are embedded before controls are established.
options.C: The Sustainability Manager's concern falls under the risk management approach, not the quality management approach, and should have been captured in the risk register during initiation. The CEO of BuildyBrick's variation order suggestion is a contractual matter entirely outside the scope of PRINCE2 management approaches.
options.D: Both concerns should have been raised directly with the Louistown City Council at corporate level, since sustainability requirements derive from the 'green town' status awarded externally and the commercial relationship with BuildyBrick was established at portfolio level before the project began.

rationale: Option B is correct. The RACI chart for initiating a project (Table 15.2) shows the Project Manager as Responsible (R) for 'agree the management approaches', with the Project Executive Accountable (A) and Senior Supplier Consulted (C). The CEO of BuildyBrick, as Senior Supplier, should be consulted on the change management approach to reflect commercial realities such as variation orders. The Sustainability Manager, fulfilling project assurance, is also Consulted (C) on management approaches. The quality management approach must incorporate sustainability requirements per the 'green town' status. Furthermore, Table 15.3 confirms that during initiation the quality management approach is created using quality specifications from product descriptions, and the benefits/sustainability management approach supports the business case — both should embed sustainability from the outset. Option A is incorrect because the Project Executive is Accountable (A), not Responsible (R), for agreeing management approaches; the Project Manager holds Responsible (R). Moreover, supplier input into management approaches is explicitly expected during initiation per the RACI table. Option C is incorrect because sustainability quality requirements are directly relevant to the quality management approach (as confirmed in Table 15.3, which states product descriptions' quality specifications help create the quality management approach), not solely the risk management approach. While risks about sustainability also exist, the quality management approach is the primary vehicle here. Option D is incorrect because management approaches are internal project governance documents created by the Project Manager during initiation. The corporate/portfolio layer (Louistown City Council) is not involved in creating these documents; the RACI table shows they are not listed for this activity. The concerns should be addressed within the project management team structure.

topic: Controlling a Stage - Escalating Issues and Risks
question: During Stage 3 (construction) of the LouisShopping project, the Principal Electrical Building Services Engineer (Team Manager for electrical work) has identified that the underground parking excavation has caused unforeseen structural vibration, placing several historical buildings at risk. The Heritage Director confirms this was not anticipated in the risk register. The Project Manager estimates that resolving the issue will require an additional six weeks and £400,000, which exceeds the stage cost and time tolerances agreed with the Project Board. The Project Manager has begun compiling an exception report. According to PRINCE2, which of the following sequences of actions BEST reflects the recommended approach for the Project Manager?

options.A: Update the project log to record the issue, immediately suspend all excavation work packages, issue the completed exception report to the Project Board, then await their decision before taking any further action or alerting the project board informally.
options.B: Evaluate the stage status first to understand the full extent of the deviation, provide an early informal notification to the Project Board that an exception situation is forecast, then produce and submit the formal exception report containing the situation, recovery options, and a recommendation for a course of action.
options.C: Raise the issue directly with the Louistown City Council (corporate layer) since the risk involves historical buildings protected under public interest, update the risk register, and only involve the Project Board after the Council has provided guidance on acceptable recovery options.
options.D: The Project Manager should take corrective action within the stage to resolve the issue using the change budget, update the stage plan and issue register accordingly, and report the resolution in the next highlight report, only escalating if the corrective action itself fails.

rationale: Option B is correct. Section 16.4.7 explicitly states: 'As it may take some time to gather the information to create an exception report, it is recommended to alert the project board as early as possible. Therefore, the project manager may wish to execute this activity in two steps: an early notification of the forecast exception situation to the project board so that they are prepared, followed by the supporting information in an exception report.' The exception report should contain 'the situation, options, and the recommendation for a course of action.' Section 16.4.4 also directs that evaluate stage status activity should precede escalation, giving the Project Manager an overall picture. The RACI table (Table 16.2) confirms the Project Manager is Responsible (R) for 'escalate issues and risks'. Option A is incorrect because PRINCE2 does not recommend suspending work packages unilaterally before the Project Board has been informed or has made a decision. Furthermore, the recommended approach is to alert the Project Board early (informally first), then follow with the formal exception report — not to withhold all communication until the report is complete. Option C is incorrect because the Project Manager reports to the Project Board, not directly to the corporate layer (Louistown City Council). The directing a project process (Chapter 14) shows that the Project Board is the interface between the project and the business/corporate layer. Bypassing the Project Board to go directly to the Council is not the PRINCE2 approach. Option D is incorrect because the scenario explicitly states the deviation exceeds stage cost and time tolerances agreed with the Project Board. Section 16.4.7 states 'This activity applies where any corrective action within the project manager's control would not save the stage from going beyond the tolerances agreed.' Once tolerances are forecast to be exceeded, escalation is mandatory, not optional. Using the change budget for corrective action is only appropriate for issues within tolerance.

topic: Managing Product Delivery - Accepting and Executing a Work Package
question: At the start of Stage 2 (design), the Project Manager issues a work package description to the Principal Electrical Building Services Engineer covering the electrical systems design for LouisShopping, including requirements for the latest technology systems aligned with the 'green town' sustainability commitments. The Principal Electrical Building Services Engineer reviews the work package and believes that the tolerances set are too tight given the complexity of integrating sustainable technology, and that the timeline does not allow for mandatory regulatory sign-off on specialist electrical systems. Which of the following actions BEST describes what the Principal Electrical Building Services Engineer should do at this stage?

options.A: Accept the work package as issued since it has been authorized by the Project Manager, and raise the concerns about tolerances and regulatory sign-off as issues in the issue register once work has commenced, updating the Project Manager via the next checkpoint report.
options.B: Refuse to accept the work package and escalate directly to the CEO of BuildyBrick (Senior Supplier) to renegotiate the commercial terms of the contract, as the tolerances imposed by the Project Manager are unrealistic for specialist electrical engineering work.
options.C: Produce a team plan to show which products can be completed within the given constraints, consult with project assurance (supplier) to confirm viability and relevant supplier standards, and raise concerns about the tolerances and regulatory sign-off with the Project Manager before agreeing to deliver the work package.
options.D: Accept the work package provisionally and immediately begin work to maximize the time available, whilst simultaneously preparing a formal exception report to the Project Board explaining why the tolerances cannot be met, bypassing the Project Manager to ensure the Project Board is informed without delay.

rationale: Option C is correct. Section 17.4.1 (Accept a work package) states that recommended actions for each team manager include: 'Produce a team plan to show which products can be completed within the given constraints' and 'Consult with project assurance (supplier) to confirm that the team plan is viable and in accordance with the relevant supplier standards.' The section also states: 'There should be an agreement between the project manager and the team manager as to what is to be delivered before a work package is allocated to a team. This should cover the reporting requirements, the constraints that apply, any procedures to be applied, and whether the requirements of the work package are reasonable and can be achieved.' The Principal Electrical Building Services Engineer should raise the viability concerns before agreeing to deliver, not after accepting. Option A is incorrect because PRINCE2 emphasises that agreement should be reached before the work package is accepted. Accepting a work package the team manager believes cannot be delivered within tolerances, then raising concerns only via checkpoint reports after work has started, undermines the purpose of the accept a work package activity and the principle of manage by exception. Option B is incorrect because the Principal Electrical Building Services Engineer, as Team Manager, reports to the Project Manager (not directly to the Senior Supplier for operational matters). While the CEO of BuildyBrick may ultimately be involved commercially, the correct channel is to raise concerns with the Project Manager during the acceptance discussion. Section 17.2 warns against 'micromanagement' but equally requires proper agreement before commencement. Option D is incorrect because Team Managers do not produce exception reports to the Project Board. Exception reports are produced by the Project Manager (per section 16.4.7 and Table 16.2). Team Managers raise issues with the Project Manager (section 17.4.2), who then decides whether to escalate. Beginning work before the work package is properly agreed also contradicts section 17.4.1.

topic: Managing a Stage Boundary - Updating the Business Case and Requesting the Next Stage
question: LouisShopping is approaching the end of Stage 3 (construction). During the stage, significant additional costs were incurred due to the unexpected structural vibration issue near historical buildings, and the Sustainability Manager has advised that achieving 'green town' compliance will require an upgraded drainage system not included in the original scope. The Regeneration Manager (Senior User) is concerned that the project may no longer deliver value within the original financial parameters. The Project Manager is preparing to manage the stage boundary. Which of the following actions BEST represents how the Project Manager should address the business case at this stage?

options.A: The Project Manager should update the business case to reflect the additional costs and the new drainage scope, but only after the next stage plan for Stage 4 has been fully approved by the Project Board, as the business case update is a consequential output of stage planning rather than an input to the stage boundary request.
options.B: The Project Manager should assess the project's risks using the project log to determine the aggregated risk exposure, examine whether the final implementation date has changed, update the benefits management approach with results from benefits management actions during the stage, and revise the business case ready for the Project Board's approval before requesting the next stage.
options.C: The Regeneration Manager, as Senior User, should be given responsibility for updating the business case since they represent the users who will ultimately determine whether the shopping centre delivers value, with the Project Manager only providing cost and schedule data as inputs.
options.D: The Project Manager should raise a request for change to add the upgraded drainage system to the scope, update only the risk register to reflect the cost overrun risks, and defer the business case update until the closing a project process, when a full review of all benefits can be conducted alongside the end project report.

rationale: Option B is correct. Section 18.4.4 (Update the business case) explicitly states the recommended actions for the Project Manager include: 'Check whether there have been any changes to the risk appetite and risk capacity of the organisations involved and whether risk tolerances need to be redefined'; 'Assess the project's risks using the project log to ascertain the aggregated risk exposure for the project'; 'Update the benefits management approach with the results from any benefits management actions undertaken during the stage'; 'Examine and review the project plan to see whether the final implementation date of the project has changed'; and 'Revise the business case and, if necessary, the benefits management approach, ready for the project board's approval.' The RACI table (Table 18.2) confirms the Project Manager is Responsible (R) for 'Update the business case' with the Project Executive Accountable (A) and Senior User (Regeneration Manager) and Senior Supplier Consulted (C). This updated business case is a critical input to 'Request the next stage' (section 18.4.6). Option A is incorrect because updating the business case is not consequential to stage plan approval — it is a prerequisite. Section 18.4.6 states the Project Manager should 'seek approval from the project board for the exception plan or stage plan and, if appropriate, the revised project plan, the revised benefits management approach, and the revised business case.' The business case must be revised before requesting authorization for the next stage, not after. Option C is incorrect because the RACI table (Table 18.2) clearly shows the Project Manager is Responsible (R) for updating the business case, not the Senior User. While the Regeneration Manager is Consulted (C), responsibility lies with the Project Manager. Section 18.4.4 also notes that 'as the project executive is accountable for the business case, the project manager should consult with them when reviewing and updating the business case.' Option D is incorrect because deferring the business case update until closing a project is contrary to PRINCE2's principle of continued business justification. Section 18.4.4 exists specifically to ensure the business case is updated at each stage boundary. A request for change for the drainage system should follow the issue management approach (change management approach), but this does not replace the need to update the business case. Risk register updates alone are insufficient — the full business case must be revised.

topic: Closing a Project – End Project Report & Business Case
question:
The LouisShopping project has reached the end of Stage 5 (demobilisation) and is preparing for planned closure. The Project Manager is drafting the end project report. The business case was baselined at the end of the initiation stage. Several approved changes were made during Stage 3, and one sustainability target relating to embodied carbon in materials was not fully achieved, resulting in a concession.

Which of the following statements BEST describes how the business case should be handled during the closing a project process?

options.A: The business case should be reviewed against its original baselined version only, without reference to approved changes, because the baseline is the definitive measure of project success.
options.B: The business case should be updated to reflect actual costs and benefits achieved, a forecast of benefits yet to be realized, and the concession granted for the unmet sustainability target, then confirmed by the Project Executive.
options.C: The business case should be closed without further update because the project product has been handed over to the Louistown City Council and all further benefit tracking becomes the responsibility of the Portfolio Director outside the project.
options.D: The business case should be updated only if the total project cost has exceeded the approved budget tolerance; otherwise the baselined version is sufficient for the end project report.

rationale: Option B is correct. PRINCE2 7 states that during closing a project, the business case is updated to reflect actual costs and benefits achieved, as well as a forecast of benefits yet to be realized (such as long-term footfall and commercial revenue that cannot yet be measured). The concession for the unmet sustainability target must also be recorded. The Project Executive (representing Louistown City Council) retains accountability for the business case and must confirm the final version. Option A is incorrect because PRINCE2 requires the project manager to review both the original baselined PID AND approved changes when evaluating performance at closure – solely referencing the original baseline would ignore legitimate scope and cost changes approved during delivery. Option C is incorrect because PRINCE2 explicitly states the business case must be evaluated and updated at closure, not simply closed without update; post-project benefit tracking responsibilities are recorded in the benefits management approach and passed on, but the business case itself must first be finalised within the project. Option D is incorrect because the business case must be updated at closure regardless of whether tolerances were breached; it is not conditional on a budget overrun. Updating it is a standard closing a project activity.

topic: Closing a Project – RACI and Responsibilities
question:
The LouisShopping project is in the closing a project process. The following activities need to be completed:

1. Confirming that all planned products in the product register have been delivered and accepted.
2. Preparing follow-on action recommendations for residual risks relating to the underground car park structure.
3. Briefing the Project Board with a summary of project performance and requesting formal project closure.
4. Checking that post-project benefits reviews are planned to take place for benefits that can only be confirmed after the shopping centre has been in operational use.

According to the PRINCE2 7 RACI chart for closing a project, which combination CORRECTLY identifies who is RESPONSIBLE for activities 2 and 4 respectively?

options.A: Activity 2: Senior Supplier (CEO of BuildyBrick); Activity 4: Senior User (Business Opportunities Director or Regeneration Manager).
options.B: Activity 2: Project Manager; Activity 4: Senior User (Business Opportunities Director or Regeneration Manager).
options.C: Activity 2: Project Manager; Activity 4: Project Manager.
options.D: Activity 2: Senior Supplier (CEO of BuildyBrick); Activity 4: Project Manager.

rationale: Option B is correct. According to PRINCE2 7 section 19.5.2, the Project Manager is responsible for preparing follow-on action recommendations, including those for residual risks (Activity 2). These recommendations capture uncompleted work, issues, and risks (such as long-term structural monitoring of the underground car park) to be transferred to the business. For Activity 4, PRINCE2 7 section 19.5.2 explicitly states that 'checking that post-project benefits reviews are planned to take place may be undertaken by the Senior User in the confirm project acceptance activity.' Therefore the Senior User (represented by the Business Opportunities Director or Regeneration Manager) holds this responsibility. Option A is incorrect because while the CEO of BuildyBrick (Senior Supplier) is consulted on many activities, they are not responsible for preparing follow-on action recommendations – that responsibility belongs to the Project Manager. Option C is incorrect because although the Project Manager has overall responsibility for the closing a project process, PRINCE2 7 specifically delegates the checking of post-project benefits review planning to the Senior User within the confirm project acceptance activity. Assigning both to the Project Manager ignores this tailoring note. Option D is incorrect for the same reason as A regarding Activity 2, and assigning Activity 4 to the Project Manager contradicts the specific PRINCE2 7 guidance that this check may be performed by the Senior User.

topic: Closing a Project – Management Products and the Project Log
question:
The LouisShopping project is approaching planned closure at the end of Stage 5. The Project Manager must ensure that all management products are correctly reviewed and closed. The Sustainability Manager has identified three residual risks that must be transferred to the council's facilities management team. The Heritage Director has confirmed that a minor excavation-related crack in a neighbouring listed building was recorded as an issue but has been resolved and closed. The quality register shows all Stage 5 quality activities were completed.

Considering the application of the practices to the closing a project process, which of the following actions represents the MOST COMPLETE and CORRECT set of actions the Project Manager should take regarding the project log?

options.A: Close the risk register after eliminating Stage 5 risks; close the issue register after confirming the crack issue is resolved; close the quality register after confirming Stage 5 quality activities are complete; review the daily log and lessons log for input to the end project report; then close the daily log and lessons log.
options.B: Transfer the three residual risks to the facilities management team by keeping the risk register open for six months after project closure to allow the new owners to update it; close all other registers at project closure.
options.C: Close the risk register only after all residual risks have been fully resolved by the facilities management team; retain the issue register open to monitor the cracked listed building for 12 months post-closure.
options.D: Review the risk register to transfer residual risks and provide input to lessons and the end project report; close the risk register; review and close the issue register; review and close the quality register; review the daily log and lessons log for input to the end project report; close the daily log and lessons log.

rationale: Option D is correct. PRINCE2 7 Table 19.3 (Application of practices to closing a project) specifies precisely what must happen to each element of the project log at closure. The risk register is reviewed to eliminate final stage risks, transfer any residual risks (such as the three sustainability-related risks) to the business (follow-on action recommendations), and provide input to lessons and the end project report – then it is closed. The issue register is reviewed to eliminate final stage issues, capture any follow-on actions, and provide input to the end project report – then it is closed. The quality register is checked to confirm all required quality activities were performed and to provide input to the end project report – then it is closed. The daily log and lessons log are reviewed for input to the end project report and then closed. Option A is largely correct but omits the critical step of transferring residual risks via follow-on action recommendations before closing the risk register, and does not mention closing the issue register – it only references the crack issue. Option B is incorrect because PRINCE2 does not require the risk register to remain open after project closure; residual risks are transferred to the business through follow-on action recommendations and the risk register is then closed within the project. Option C is incorrect because PRINCE2 does not require the project to maintain open registers until all operational risks are resolved; that would blur the project boundary. Residual risks are documented in follow-on action recommendations and handed to the business.

topic: Closing a Project – Premature Closure and Follow-On Actions
question:
During Stage 3 of the LouisShopping project, a significant structural survey reveals that the ground conditions beneath the proposed underground car park are far worse than anticipated. The revised cost estimate to safely construct the underground car park exceeds the total remaining project budget by 40%, making the business case no longer viable. The Portfolio Director instructs the Project Board to trigger premature closure of the project.

Which of the following actions is the MOST APPROPRIATE set of activities for the Project Manager to carry out when preparing for premature closure?

options.A: Immediately archive all project documentation, notify BuildyBrick to cease all work, and issue a final highlight report to the Project Board explaining the reasons for closure without producing an end project report, as this is only required for planned closure.
options.B: Prepare follow-on action recommendations covering uncompleted work, outstanding issues (including the ground condition structural risk), and residual risks; evaluate the project against the version of the PID used to authorise it; prepare an end project report; review the project log for lessons; and request premature closure from the Project Board.
options.C: Prepare an exception report for the Project Board detailing the 40% cost overrun, request an exception plan to cover the remaining project work at reduced scope, and continue the project without the underground car park element to preserve the business case.
options.D: Transfer all outstanding issues and risks directly to the Portfolio Director's risk register without preparing follow-on action recommendations, as the Portfolio Director has already decided to close the project and the project-level documentation is no longer relevant.

rationale: Option B is correct. PRINCE2 7 section 19.4 states that when a project is closed prematurely, the closing a project process is still carried out. The Project Manager must prepare follow-on action recommendations covering any uncompleted work, outstanding issues, and risks (including the critical ground condition findings that triggered closure, and any structural risks to neighbouring heritage buildings). The project must still be evaluated against the baselined PID to assess performance and understand what was achieved, an end project report must be produced, lessons must be captured from the project log, and the Project Board must formally be asked to authorise premature closure. Option A is incorrect because PRINCE2 7 requires an end project report even for premature closure – it is not exclusive to planned closure. Issuing only a highlight report does not fulfil this obligation, and simply archiving without the proper closing activities bypasses formal governance. Option C is incorrect because the Portfolio Director has already instructed premature closure due to the business case being unviable. An exception report and exception plan are mechanisms used when a stage or project plan is forecast to exceed tolerance but the project is expected to continue; they are not the appropriate response when the business justification has been fundamentally invalidated and closure has been directed. Option D is incorrect because PRINCE2 requires the Project Manager to formally prepare follow-on action recommendations as a defined management product during premature closure. These recommendations ensure that residual risks, uncompleted work, and outstanding issues are properly documented and handed over; they cannot simply be verbally transferred. The project log must also be reviewed for lessons.

topic: Closing a Project – End Project Report
question: The LouisShopping project has reached the end of Stage 5 (demobilization) and the Project Manager is preparing the end project report. The construction of the shopping centre is complete, but two minor car park lighting systems were delivered with a concession accepted by the Project Board, and several sustainability performance metrics will only be measurable after six months of operational use. Which of the following statements BEST describes what the end project report should contain in this situation?

options.A: A comparison of actual project performance against the version of the project initiation documentation used to authorise the project, including a summary of follow-on action recommendations covering the concessions and the post-project sustainability metrics to be confirmed later.
options.B: A forecast of all future benefits to be realised by the Louistown City Council, replacing the business case content, since the project is now closing and the business case is no longer relevant to project operations.
options.C: A record of only the products that were fully completed to specification, omitting the concessions on car park lighting to avoid drawing attention to deviations that have already been formally accepted by the Project Board.
options.D: A full update of the risk register to transfer all residual risks to operational teams, which constitutes the primary purpose of the end project report at this point in the project lifecycle.

rationale: A is correct: The end project report's purpose is to review how the project performed against the version of the PID used to authorise it. It must include a performance review (covering the business case, project objectives, and team performance), a product review (including concessions and off-specifications), and a summary of follow-on action recommendations. The concessions on the car park lighting and the sustainability metrics that cannot yet be confirmed are classic examples of content required in follow-on action recommendations, ensuring the business knows what residual actions remain post-project. B is incorrect: The end project report does not replace the business case. The business case is updated during closing a project to reflect actual costs and a forecast of benefits yet to be realised, but it remains a separate management product. The end project report reviews performance against the PID, not replacement of the business case. C is incorrect: The end project report must include a product review covering concessions and off-specifications. Even if the Project Board has formally accepted a concession, it must be recorded in the end project report to give a complete and accurate account of project performance. Omitting accepted concessions would undermine the integrity of the report. D is incorrect: While the risk register is reviewed during closing a project to transfer residual risks to the business and provide input to the end project report, updating the risk register is not the primary purpose of the end project report. The risk register is a project log record; the end project report is a separate report product with its own distinct purpose.

topic: Closing a Project – RACI Responsibilities
question: The LouisShopping project is in the 'confirm project acceptance' activity within the closing a project process. The CEO of BuildyBrick has completed all contracted works and the shopping centre has been handed over. The Business Opportunities Director and the Regeneration Manager need to formally sign off acceptance. Referring to the RACI chart for closing a project, which of the following BEST describes the accountability and responsibility assignments for this activity?

options.A: The Project Executive (representing Louistown City Council) is Accountable, the CEO of BuildyBrick is Responsible, and the Business Opportunities Director and Regeneration Manager are Consulted on confirming project acceptance.
options.B: The business layer is Accountable, the CEO of BuildyBrick is Responsible for confirming acceptance, and the Project Manager, project assurance, and project support are all Informed of the outcome.
options.C: The Project Manager is Accountable for confirming project acceptance, the CEO of BuildyBrick is Responsible, and the Project Executive is only Informed once acceptance is confirmed.
options.D: The business layer is Accountable, the CEO of BuildyBrick is Responsible, and the Project Manager is Consulted throughout the confirm project acceptance activity.

rationale: B is correct: According to the RACI chart for closing a project (Table 19.2), for the 'Confirm project acceptance only' activity, the business layer is Accountable (A), the Senior Supplier (CEO of BuildyBrick) is Responsible (R), and the Project Manager, project assurance, and project support are all Informed (I). The Senior User (Business Opportunities Director and Regeneration Manager) are Consulted (C). This reflects that formal acceptance is driven at the project board level with the supplier being responsible for confirming the product meets agreed criteria. A is incorrect: While the Project Executive is part of the business layer which is Accountable, the RACI chart shows the Senior Supplier (CEO of BuildyBrick) as Responsible, not just Consulted. Additionally, the Senior Users (Business Opportunities Director and Regeneration Manager) are Consulted, not simply signing off as described. The description in option A misrepresents the RACI assignments. C is incorrect: The Project Manager is not Accountable for the confirm project acceptance activity. The business layer (project board level) holds accountability. The Project Manager is Informed for this activity, not Accountable. This is a key distinction in PRINCE2 governance. D is incorrect: The RACI chart shows the Project Manager as Informed (I) during confirm project acceptance, not Consulted (C). The distinction between Consulted and Informed is significant: Consulted means two-way communication is required, while Informed means one-way notification only. The Project Manager's role at this point is to receive information, not to be actively consulted.

topic: Closing a Project – Practices Applied
question: At the end of the LouisShopping project, the Project Manager is reviewing all project logs and registers as part of the closing a project process. The Heritage Director has raised a late concern that records of all underground excavation decisions and findings should be preserved for future reference by the city council. The Sustainability Manager has flagged that the sustainability management approach needs to be checked for post-project review responsibilities. Which TWO management products are MOST relevant to address these specific concerns during closing a project?

options.A: The digital and data management approach (to determine how project data, including excavation records, will be archived) and the sustainability management approach (to define post-project sustainability review responsibilities).
options.B: The risk register (to capture the Heritage Director's concern as a new risk) and the quality register (to record the Sustainability Manager's findings as a quality control activity).
options.C: The lessons report (to document the Heritage Director's concern as a lesson learned) and the benefits management approach (to assign the Sustainability Manager's post-project responsibilities to the senior user).
options.D: The issue register (to formally raise the Heritage Director's concern as a new issue) and the communication management approach (to notify all stakeholders of post-project sustainability review requirements).

rationale: A is correct: According to Table 19.3 (Application of the practices to the closing a project process), the Progress practice specifies that the digital and data management approach is reviewed for any requirements relating to project closure, specifically addressing how and where project data will be archived or used. This directly addresses the Heritage Director's concern about preserving excavation records. The Business Case practice specifies that the benefits management approach and sustainability management approach are reviewed and updated for post-project responsibilities, which directly addresses the Sustainability Manager's concern. B is incorrect: While the risk register is reviewed during closing a project to transfer residual risks, capturing a late concern about data archiving as a new risk would not be the most appropriate response at project closure. Similarly, the quality register captures quality control activities, not sustainability management responsibilities. These are not the most relevant management products for the two specific concerns raised. C is incorrect: A lessons report could capture learning from the excavation work, but it does not address the operational need to preserve excavation records for future city council reference. The benefits management approach does address post-project responsibilities, but it is the sustainability management approach specifically that covers sustainability review responsibilities. This option conflates two different management products and misapplies their purposes. D is incorrect: While an issue register could record the Heritage Director's concern, at project closure the more appropriate vehicle is the digital and data management approach, which is specifically designed to govern how project data is retained. The communication management approach governs stakeholder communication methods and frequencies, not post-project sustainability review assignments, making this option a misapplication of both management products.

topic: Closing a Project – Follow-on Action Recommendations
question: The LouisShopping shopping centre has been handed over to the Louistown City Council operations team at the end of Stage 5. The Project Manager is preparing follow-on action recommendations as part of planned closure. Several items remain outstanding: (1) two automated door mechanisms failed their final inspection and were accepted as off-specifications with a concession; (2) a request for change to add electric vehicle charging points was not progressed due to budget constraints; (3) residual risks relating to the structural integrity of the adjacent historical buildings will need ongoing monitoring by the city council. Which statement BEST describes how these items should be handled in the follow-on action recommendations?

options.A: All three items should be included in the follow-on action recommendations: the off-specification concessions and uncompleted change request are uncompleted work or issues, and the residual structural integrity risks should be transferred to the business for ongoing management.
options.B: Only the residual risks relating to the historical buildings need to be included in the follow-on action recommendations, as the off-specification concessions have already been formally accepted and the change request was formally rejected, so neither requires further action.
options.C: The follow-on action recommendations should only address items that were not formally decided during the project; since the concessions and the rejected change request were both formally decided by the Project Board, they should be excluded from the follow-on action recommendations.
options.D: The follow-on action recommendations are solely the responsibility of the CEO of BuildyBrick to prepare, as the contractor holds accountability for the quality of delivered products and must advise the city council on all outstanding matters.

rationale: A is correct: PRINCE2 7 specifies that follow-on action recommendations should include any uncompleted work, issues, and risks. The two automated door mechanisms delivered with a concession represent uncompleted work or off-specifications that the operational team needs to be aware of and potentially act upon. The electric vehicle charging point request for change, not progressed due to budget constraints, represents uncompleted work that may be picked up by a future project or operational initiative.
